roots.title: Neural Networks, Course Evaluations, and Logic Gates
roots.summary: The segment introduces neural networks, clarifying their absence from the final exam, labs, or homework. It emphasizes the importance of course evaluations, which account for 25% of the final grade, with an extra 0.5% credit for 90% participation. The structure and function of biological neurons are discussed, covering components like dendrites and synapses, and comparing them to artificial neurons, explaining input weighting and processing. Neural networks are described as creating linear functions from weighted inputs to produce activation values, with activation functions constraining outputs. Additionally, a probabilistic view on solution spaces is presented, illustrating multiple correct solutions with four equations and three unknowns. The discussion includes the requirement for positive values of teta variables, demonstrating how teta-1 and teta-2 can sum to zero under specific conditions. Lastly, the construction of logic gates, including OR and XOR gates, is explored, with examples illustrating functional completeness in Boolean operations.

children.title: Neural Networks Introduction and Course Evaluations
children.summary: The segment introduces neural networks, clarifying that they will not be on the final exam, labs, or homework. Demonstrations of neural networks are provided. Course logistics highlight the importance of course evaluations, which contribute 25% to the final grade, and an incentive of an extra 0.5% credit for 90% class participation in evaluations. The structure and function of biological neurons are discussed, detailing components like dendrites, cell body, axon, and synapses. The comparison between biological and artificial neurons is made, explaining how inputs are weighted and processed. The segment also covers how neural networks create linear functions from inputs multiplied by weights to produce an activation value, detailing the role of activation functions in constraining outputs.

children.title: Introduction to Neural Networks and Course Evaluations
children.summary: The lecture introduces neural networks, noting that this topic will not be included in the final exam, labs, or homework. The instructor and Joy provide demonstrations of neural networks. Additionally, course logistics are covered, highlighting the significance of course evaluations, which account for 25% of the final course credit. If 90% of the class participates in both evaluations, those who complete them will receive an extra 0.5% credit.

children.title: Introduction to Neural Networks and Course Logistics
children.summary: The lecture introduces neural networks, emphasizing that the topic will not be on the final exam or any lab or homework. It includes a demo by the instructor and a subsequent demo by Joy. Additionally, course logistics are discussed, particularly the importance of course evaluations, which can contribute 25% towards final course credit. If 90% of the class participates in both evaluations, those who fill them out will receive an additional 0.5% credit.

children.title: Introduction to Neural Networks and Neuron Structure
children.summary: The lecture introduces neural networks, emphasizing their inspiration from the brain's structure and function. It highlights the complexity of the brain, which contains around 10 billion neurons communicating rapidly through spikes of information. The discussion covers the structure of biological neurons, detailing the roles of dendrites, cell body, axon, and synapses in signal transmission. Dendrites receive signals chemically, the cell body processes these signals to determine activation, and axons transmit outputs to other neurons. The segment also compares biological neurons to artificial neurons, explaining how inputs are weighted and processed to produce outputs.

children.title: Introduction to Neural Networks and Brain Structure
children.summary: The lecture introduces neural networks, aiming to inspire confidence in students to explore the topic further. It begins with a primer on the brain's structure, highlighting its complexity and the rudimentary understanding of neuron functions. The discussion emphasizes the inspiration drawn from cognitive science and neuroscience in developing neural networks, noting that the brain consists of approximately 10 billion neurons that communicate rapidly through spikes of information. This communication involves hundreds of operations per second, forming the basis for neural network architectures.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: One of the things that we kept hearing in prior semesters is introduction to neural networks. So there you go. This is a neural network lecture. So after this lecture, I'm hoping that you all have the confidence to actually go and learn more on your own, more about neural networks and more about deep learning. So this is just going to be a segue into it. So I'm going to start by talking a little bit about Some kind of a primer into how we can think about neural networks. I'm going to talk about brain first. So what is the structure of the brain we're not going to go into the details of how the brain works, but just realizing that brain is a very complex phenomenon and then the understanding of how brain works and how each individual neuron actually works is very kind of our understanding is very rudimentary and then. what we're trying to get inspiration from in building neural networks is different concepts in brain. And there is a lot of collaboration between cognitive science, neuroscience, and also getting inspiration from those as people started building neural networks in different deep architectures that are now kind of very famous in the field. So a neuron, like brain has 10 billions of neurons, The switching time between the neurons is very fast. Each neuron tries to fire some kind of information, and then the other neurons is going to receive those spikes. And then these spikes are going to be propagated through the entire network of the brain and all of the neurons in the brain. There are generally hundreds of operations being done per second. children.title: Neural Network Simulation and Neuron Structure
children.summary: The segment discusses the structure and function of biological neurons and how they relate to artificial neurons in neural networks. It explains the role of dendrites, which receive signals through chemical reactions, and the cell body, which aggregates information to determine if the neuron will activate. The axon transmits signals to synapses, which connect to other neurons. Synapses can be excitatory, leading to the activation of the next neuron, or inhibitory, indicating insufficient signals for activation. The goal is to mimic these biological processes in artificial neurons.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's how you can also think about how we want to make that the way that we're simulating neural networks is kind of working in the same way. But thinking about one individual neuron and different parts of an individual, like biological neuron, that we are trying to simulate in an artificial neuron, is kind of different pieces. So the different pieces of a neuron, there's like, den writes that neurons have, and these den writes are actually different branches that reach signal. signal. So you're thinking about then rice being here. So it is kind of spread that branch like it receives the signals through different chemical reactions that that appears between multiple neurons that we have. There is so more cell body which is kind of a unit that tries to collect information and aggregate information and decide whether the neuron is going to be activated or not activated. So you can think about it as kind of a processing unit in a biological neuron that we have. Once the process is actually done and then axon is going to transmit all of those signals to the synopsis and these synopsis are the connections that we're making or the neuron is making two other neurons to pass information along. So you can think about these synopsis as different parts in different connections between different neurons that that either is going to send a chemical reaction to activate the next neuron or not activate the next neuron. So, we are calling these synopsis as excitatory synopsis or inhibitory synopsis. Excitedory means that the next neuron is going to fire. It has received indication that some kind of stimulation has happened in the previous neuron or inhibitory means that there is lack of enough information or signal in the previous neurons that there's no enough signal or chemical reactions that can be passed through the entire network. So what we're actually trying to mimic in an artificial neuron is exactly the same thing. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

There is so more cell body which is kind of a unit that tries to collect information and aggregate information and decide whether the neuron is going to be activated or not activated. So you can think about it as kind of a processing unit in a biological neuron that we have. Once the process is actually done and then axon is going to transmit all of those signals to the synopsis and these synopsis are the connections that we're making or the neuron is making two other neurons to pass information along. So you can think about these synopsis as different parts in different connections between different neurons that that either is going to send a chemical reaction to activate the next neuron or not activate the next neuron. So, we are calling these synopsis as excitatory synopsis or inhibitory synopsis. Excitedory means that the next neuron is going to fire. It has received indication that some kind of stimulation has happened in the previous neuron or inhibitory means that there is lack of enough information or signal in the previous neurons that there's no enough signal or chemical reactions that can be passed through the entire network. So what we're actually trying to mimic in an artificial neuron is exactly the same thing.

children.title: From Biological to Artificial Neurons
children.summary: Biological neurons consist of several key components: inputs received through dendrites, a processing unit within the cell body, and outputs transmitted via axons. The summation of inputs occurs in the cell body before the neuron sends signals through synapses to communicate with other neurons.

children.title: From Biological to Artificial Neurons
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of information transmission in neurons, comparing biological neurons to artificial neurons. It explains how dendrites serve as inputs that receive information through synaptic connections, leading to a weighted combination of inputs calculated by the neuron to determine its output. The output is a function applied to these weighted inputs. The simplest model for understanding artificial neurons is likened to linear regression, highlighting the foundational concepts of inputs and outputs in neural processing.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We have a processing unit and then we're trying to actually transmit those information that be processed into the next neurons. And we want to make sure that as we process the information, as we have some kind of activation or information that we're trying to share with other neurons, those activations are also So kind of working in the same way, they're excited or in inhibitory meaning that the next neuron is going to be activated or not activated. So what we're trying to do, again, artificial neuron has a similar kind of reaction. So remember that we had dendrites, so these dendrites as inputs. So you're thinking about these dendrites inputs receiving information through the synaptic connections to the previous neurons. So here we also have the same thing. So we have inputs going into the neuron that we have. And then the neuron actually is going to use it similar cell body in order to calculate a weighted combination of those inputs or those reactions that it has received in order to decide what the output is going to be. And the output is going to be a function that is applied to a weighted combination of inputs that it has received. So these equations you've seen them before. So we're going to formally define what function F is going to look like whether those parameters, whether those inputs, and so on and so forth in the first part of today's lecture. So let's go through the very first and the simplest way that we can think about the neuron. We can think about the neuron as just the artificial neuron as just a simple linear regression. It has inputs going into the neuron, right? And the simplest thing that we learned was linear regression. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

leaf_chunk.audio: We have a processing unit and then we're trying to actually transmit those information that be processed into the next neurons. And we want to make sure that as we process the information, as we have some kind of activation or information that we're trying to share with other neurons, those activations are also So kind of working in the same way, they're excited or in inhibitory meaning that the next neuron is going to be activated or not activated. So what we're trying to do, again, artificial neuron has a similar kind of reaction. So remember that we had dendrites, so these dendrites as inputs. So you're thinking about these dendrites inputs receiving information through the synaptic connections to the previous neurons. So here we also have the same thing. So we have inputs going into the neuron that we have. And then the neuron actually is going to use it similar cell body in order to calculate a weighted combination of those inputs or those reactions that it has received in order to decide what the output is going to be. And the output is going to be a function that is applied to a weighted combination of inputs that it has received. So these equations you've seen them before. So we're going to formally define what function F is going to look like whether those parameters, whether those inputs, and so on and so forth in the first part of today's lecture. So let's go through the very first and the simplest way that we can think about the neuron. We can think about the neuron as just the artificial neuron as just a simple linear regression. It has inputs going into the neuron, right? And the simplest thing that we learned was linear regression.

children.title: Linear Functions and Neuron Activation Overview
children.summary: The segment explains how neural networks process inputs by multiplying them with weights to create a linear function, resulting in an 'activation' value that determines neuron activation. It highlights the structure of a simple artificial neuron, focusing on the weighted combination of inputs, including a bias term. The neuron computes a summation of these weighted inputs, which is then passed through an activation function that applies thresholding or specific functions to control the output. The activation function ensures outputs are constrained, typically between 0 and 1 or -1 and 1, indicating inhibition or excitation of the neuron.

children.title: Linear Function and Neuron Activation
children.summary: The segment discusses how inputs are processed in a neural network by multiplying them with weights to create a linear function. This function produces a value termed 'activation', which indicates whether a neuron should be activated based on the weighted combination of inputs. The relationship between this process and biological neurons is highlighted, with inputs represented as x1 through xd, including a bias term.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what do we do? We have all of those inputs and then I would say that, okay, I'm going to get those inputs. I'm going to multiply these inputs with different weights. I'm going to combine them. This is a linear function and this linear function is going to give me value that for the purpose of this lecture I'm just going to call this value as an activation. So this is kind of an indication that looking at the weighted combination of those inputs that we have received either through the input or through the connections from different neurons are we going to activate this neuron or not activate this neuron. And this why has value right now is our activation for that purpose. So a very simple kind of structure. If you actually think about it in terms of like, schematically, how is going to relate to kind of a biological neuron? We have all of those inputs. So notice that I have x1 through xd. And then I also have one indication for the biosterm. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Weighted Inputs and Activation Functions in Neurons
children.summary: The segment discusses the structure of a simple artificial neuron, focusing on the weighted combination of inputs. Each input is associated with a weight, where the bias term corresponds to theta zero and other features correspond to theta one through theta d. The neuron calculates a summation of these weighted inputs, which serves as the basis for the activation function. The activation function applies thresholding or a specific function to control the output, differentiating it from merely calculating a weighted sum.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Remember the biostrict that we had. So we would augment the feature space that we have one column of one values. And then that was the input that would correspond to the bias term or the theta zero that we have. So what we're going to do is we're going to weight all of these inputs. So the weight associated to the bias term is going to be just the bias parameter theta zero. The weight associated to x1 is going to be theta one and all the way to the last feature is going to be theta d. So we're going to multiply them. So calculating a weighted kind of combination of all of these ones and then we're summing up all of these way, the multiplications that we calculated through the inputs and the parameters that we have. So that's a summation unit. Remember that I said, so more cell body is a summation and activation unit. So this is a summation piece that we're going to value the information that we're receiving from different inputs differently through the weights that we received. So the weight of tet A1, tet A2D and tet A0, they're all going to be different values that we're going learn and what we're going to do is we're calculating this weighted combination. our activation and output. So very simple artificial neuron. But again, remember that I said that a similar cell body is going to actually have summation unit plus also some activation function. So what does that activation function look like? I don't want to directly say that, okay, I'm calculating a weighted combination of inputs and there you go. This is your activation. I want to actually do some thresholding or some apply some kind of function so that I can control what the output is going to be. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Artificial Neuron and Activation Functions
children.summary: An artificial neuron consists of a summation unit and an activation function. The summation unit computes the weighted combination of inputs, and this weighted summation is then processed by the activation function to determine the neuron's activation.

children.title: Artificial Neuron and Activation Function Overview
children.summary: An artificial neuron consists of a summation unit and an activation function. The summation unit calculates the weighted combination of inputs, which is then passed to the activation function. This function controls the output range of the neuron, ensuring it does not produce values from negative infinity to positive infinity. Instead, the output is typically constrained between 0 and 1 or between -1 and 1, where -1 indicates inhibition of the neuron firing and +1 indicates excitation. The activation function is crucial for determining whether the next neuron will receive a positive value and decide to fire.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the way that that looks like is that, again, we're calculating the weighted combination of inputs that we have, right? But on top I hope that what I'm going to do here is that I'm applying this activation function, function f. So again, what that looks like, schematically in this graph is that I had this summation unit. I will calculate the output. I'll pass the output to the activation function. The activation function is now going to output a value that again, the whole idea of this activation function is that we can control what value we put. And what is the range of the values that the neuron can generate so that it cannot generate. values from negative infinity to positive infinity. So we want to make sure that the output range is always controlled. So we could probably say that the output range is going to be between 0 and 1. Or the output range of dysfunction is going to be between negative 1 and 1. Negative 1, meaning that I'm trying to inhibit the neuron from firing. Positive 1 means that I'm trying to excite the output so that the next neuron is actually going to receive a positive value and then decide whether it wants to fire or not fire, based on this feature that it received. So that's the whole purpose of having activation function. It puts a reasonable output range for a specific neuron. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

leaf_chunk.audio: So the way that that looks like is that, again, we're calculating the weighted combination of inputs that we have, right? But on top I hope that what I'm going to do here is that I'm applying this activation function, function f. So again, what that looks like, schematically in this graph is that I had this summation unit. I will calculate the output. I'll pass the output to the activation function. The activation function is now going to output a value that again, the whole idea of this activation function is that we can control what value we put. And what is the range of the values that the neuron can generate so that it cannot generate. values from negative infinity to positive infinity. So we want to make sure that the output range is always controlled. So we could probably say that the output range is going to be between 0 and 1. Or the output range of dysfunction is going to be between negative 1 and 1. Negative 1, meaning that I'm trying to inhibit the neuron from firing. Positive 1 means that I'm trying to excite the output so that the next neuron is actually going to receive a positive value and then decide whether it wants to fire or not fire, based on this feature that it received. So that's the whole purpose of having activation function. It puts a reasonable output range for a specific neuron.

children.title: Neuron Structure and Activation Functions
children.summary: An artificial neuron consists of a summation unit and an activation function. The summation unit calculates the weighted combination of inputs, which is then passed to the activation function to determine the neuron's activation. The segment discusses different types of activation functions, starting with a simple linear function where the output equals the input. It emphasizes that understanding these components is not overly complicated and encourages patience in learning about the various elements of neural networks.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then now my output is actually going to be the result of applying. This activation function to the summation value or this z-input activation that I had, and then I have y-hat value. So this whole thing is just the entire idea of neuron. So you know all the different pieces together. You have learned different kinds of ways that we're trying to build different linear models. We optimize how we can actually come up with different features, do fence the different linear models. We're going to talk a little bit about what does function, the activation function f is going to look like. So what kind of activation functions we can have. One simple way is to just say that the activation function is going to be y equal to x input in, output out. So the same input output, one to one correspondence, but we're going to talk about different kinds of activation functions that we have. referring to different concepts in neural network, but if you dig deep and then have the patience to kind of learn about all different elements, it's just very not complicated. So the opposite of the things that people would think. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Step Function Activation in Neurons
children.summary: The activation function can be defined as a simple step function, where if the input activation is greater than or equal to zero, the neuron is activated (output is 1). Conversely, if the input activation is negative, the neuron is not activated (output is 0). This process involves calculating the weighted combination of inputs, and if the result is negative, it indicates insufficient information to activate the neuron. In the context of a binary classification task with a two-dimensional dataset, this step function can be applied to determine whether data points belong to class zero or class one.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So now let's talk about the first activation. Remember just a notion of being active, being not active, having the output being equal to one or output being equal to zero. What if I decide my activation function to be just a simple step function? The way that I'm going to define this a step function is simple, that I would say that if the input activation is greater than or equal to zero, I'm going to say that the neuron is activated. If the input, the weighted combination of inputs, or the input activation is a negative value, that means that there is lack of information from the inputs for me to say that the neuron is activated. Therefore, I would say that the output is equal to zero, right? A simple step function. So what's happening here is that when you're on calculating the weighted combination of inputs and that's negative, I would say that this is you activated again the other way is going to be activating a neuron. So what's happening here is that again I do the summation as usual and then I'll pass it through activation function right now my activation function is a step function simple step function with the threshold at zero and then I'll get the output production. So what's happening after this is that if I have a data set and for simplicity, I'm assuming that my data set is a two dimensional data set. And the task that I'm going to run is a binary classification. I want to say whether the data points belong to class zero or class one. So one thing that I can do is I can actually use exactly the same method that I had, right? Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Input Activation and Decision Boundary in Neural Networks
children.summary: The input activation is calculated using the weighted combination of inputs, represented as the dot product of the theta vector and the x vector, resulting in the input activation z. The decision boundary is defined where z equals zero; points above this line are classified as class one, while those below are class zero. This illustrates the necessity of an activation function, which converts input activations into classifications, determining whether a neuron is activated and the class to which a data point belongs.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I'm going to say that I'm calculating the activation or the input activation. I'm going to calculate the weighted combination of inputs or the dot product, which means the tetah vector and the x vector that's going to give me the input activation z. And I would say that this right here is my decision boundary, where z is equal to zero. And if I apply my step function here, I would say that any point that is above on or above this line is going to be like the class one and anything below this line is going to be is going to be like the class zero. Again, this is a line of z equal to zero. Anything above the line is going to be classified as class one. Anything below it is going to be classified as class zero. So that's why we need to have an activation function or like one example of why we need to have an activation function because we want to use input activations to turn them into classifications, into decisions, whether the neuron is activated or not, or decisions into whether the data point belongs to class 0 or class 1. So this is kind of a simple way. Again, that's the kind of a way that the activation function is working. Input Z is going in. Then it decides to do the classification for us. children.title: Binary Classification and Activation Functions
children.summary: The classification process utilizes a step function with a linear decision boundary, allowing for perfect classification between two linearly separable classes. The discussion contrasts the step function with logistic regression, highlighting that both involve calculating a linear component (z) and passing it through an activation function. The step function simplifies the classification to two classes (class zero and class one), but the lecture raises a question about the limitations of the step function compared to the sigmoid function, which was preferred in prior discussions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then the way that it does the classification for us is that because the step function has a linear decision boundary, because now the characteristic for this z is that it's a linear model that I have right here. Then I have a line between the two classes. And because my data set was linearly separable, then I can actually directly cut through the two classes. Then I will have a perfect classification between the two classes. Very interesting stuff. So, this is step function is very similar to something that you've seen before. And actually, Joy and I were talking about when we were teaching you logistic regression, why did we not tell you about the step function? We could just simply say that when you're doing binary classification, you do activation, right? Because logistic regression also had the calculation of that linear part, because you had to actually calculate z and then pass z to the signal function to get the probabilities. One way of simplifying that was that, what if we told you that, OK, go ahead and calculate z, pass it through the step function, get two classes, class zero and class one. What's the issue with the step function that we did not teach you that I'm we instead talk about the sigma function. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Sigmoid Activation Function in Neural Networks
children.summary: The discussion covers the limitations of using certain classifiers, particularly regarding the differentiability of the gradient descent function at zero. It introduces the sigmoid function as an alternative that ensures differentiability while mapping inputs to a range between zero and one, representing probabilities. A threshold of 0.5 is used for classification, where values above it are classified as class 1 and below as class 0. The role of activation functions in neurons is emphasized, noting that they can be linear, step functions, or sigmoid functions. Using the sigmoid function allows neurons to output probabilities suitable for binary classification.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The issue is that if I was using that classifier and I wanted to train a classifier in terms of figuring out whether the parameters theta that I have in order to calculate z equal to the dot product within the theta vector and the x vector. The issue when you're calculating the gradient descent function is that this function is not differentiable in all points. So the derivative at zero is not the fun. So that's part of the reason that we don't necessarily use this as the function in practice because we can actually optimize using that. The other situation is that if you relax this kind of transition between the class of zero and class of one, we could actually come up with the sigmoid function. So this is another way of us trying to make sure that at zero the function is differentiable, but it is still does the same thing that it maps the inputs into the range of zero and one. So it is between zero and one. It is a probability. if we use the threshold of 0.5, anything above it is going to be classified as class 1, anything below it is going to be classified as class 0. So again, a neuron had a summation unit. It has an activation function. We talked about the activation function. It can be just a simple linear function. It can also be a step function or it can be the sigmoid function. If we use sigmoid function as the activation function, the output of each neuron is going to be between 0 and 1. It's a probability. So we can actually try to optimize the tetavalu's in a neuron so that it outputs probabilities that are reasonable and can cut through between the two classes that we have and do the binary classification for a given neuron that we have. leaf_chunk.audio: The issue is that if I was using that classifier and I wanted to train a classifier in terms of figuring out whether the parameters theta that I have in order to calculate z equal to the dot product within the theta vector and the x vector. The issue when you're calculating the gradient descent function is that this function is not differentiable in all points. So the derivative at zero is not the fun. So that's part of the reason that we don't necessarily use this as the function in practice because we can actually optimize using that. The other situation is that if you relax this kind of transition between the class of zero and class of one, we could actually come up with the sigmoid function. So this is another way of us trying to make sure that at zero the function is differentiable, but it is still does the same thing that it maps the inputs into the range of zero and one. So it is between zero and one. It is a probability. if we use the threshold of 0.5, anything above it is going to be classified as class 1, anything below it is going to be classified as class 0. So again, a neuron had a summation unit. It has an activation function. We talked about the activation function. It can be just a simple linear function. It can also be a step function or it can be the sigmoid function. If we use sigmoid function as the activation function, the output of each neuron is going to be between 0 and 1. It's a probability. So we can actually try to optimize the tetavalu's in a neuron so that it outputs probabilities that are reasonable and can cut through between the two classes that we have and do the binary classification for a given neuron that we have.

children.title: Neurons vs. Neural Networks: Limitations and Advancements
children.summary: The discussion covers the limitations of a single neuron and the necessity of neural networks, drawing an analogy between neurons and logical gates. It emphasizes the need for more complex structures to address these limitations. The segment also explores the expressiveness of artificial neurons as feature detectors and highlights the significance of deep neural networks in multi-class classification.

children.title: Neurons and Neural Networks Overview
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the limitations of a single neuron and the rationale for advancing to neural networks. It highlights the analogy between neurons and logical gates, emphasizing the need for a more complex structure to overcome these limitations. The segment also touches on the expressiveness of artificial neurons, their role as feature detectors, and the importance of deep neural networks for multi-class classification.

children.title: Simulating AND Gate with Neurons and Parameters
children.summary: The segment discusses simulating an AND gate using neurons, where the output y is 1 only if both inputs x1 and x2 are 1. A neuron is constructed with a step function activation and a threshold of zero, incorporating inputs and their theta values. It explores parameter combinations for achieving the AND gate functionality, highlighting the importance of the bias term and noting multiple correct solutions. The derivation of equations for neuron outputs is explained, including how to set input values to create necessary inequalities. Lastly, it covers conditions for activation functions in neural networks, focusing on the relationships between the parameters.

children.title: Simulating an AND Gate with Neurons
children.summary: The discussion focuses on simulating a function that maps inputs x1 and x2 to an output y, specifically corresponding to an AND gate. The output y equals 1 only when both x1 and x2 are 1, and is 0 otherwise. In the feature space, this relationship is represented with red dots indicating the output values. A simple neuron is constructed using a step function activation with a threshold of zero, incorporating inputs x1 and x2 along with their respective theta values (tetah0, tetah1, tetah2). The goal is to determine the combination of these theta values that accurately represents the AND gate behavior.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And if that's a teaser into the next part of the five minutes of the lecture that we're going to spend on. So the first thing that we want to simulate is that what if I have that function? Again, anything in machine learning is a function. So let's say that I have a function that takes in the values x1 and x2, and then it tries to map it to the output y. And the output y happens to be corresponding to the AND gate that I have. Right? So notice that the y value is only equal to 1 when both x1 and x2 are equal to 1 and in other places it's equal to zero. If I draw what's happening in my feature space is that if I plot x1 versus x2 and you notice that only in this case, y is equal to one and in other cases, y is equal to zero that I showed with the red dots. What I want to actually do is to build a simple neuron with the step function activation that has a threshold of zero obviously with those parameters that I have, right? So we always have the bias term. We always have all of the inputs going in. So I have input x1, I have input x2, because these are the two inputs that I had into the function. Then we have the corresponding tetah values, tetah1 and tetah2. What is the combination of tetah0, tetah1 and tetah2 values that is going to give me the exact. Video: 1: v

children.title: Parameters for Boolean AND Gate
children.summary: The segment discusses the characteristics of a line that separates two classes in a neural network, specifically for the Boolean AND function. It emphasizes the classification of inputs above the line as class one and those below as class zero. Various parameter combinations for achieving the AND gate functionality are suggested, including theta_0 = 0, theta_1 = -0.5, theta_2 = -0.5, and other variations. The importance of the bias term is also highlighted, indicating that multiple correct solutions exist for realizing the AND function.

So take a look at this that Kind of intuition behind it is that I'm trying to find the characteristics for the line that cuts between the two classes. and then classifies anything above the line as class one and classifies anything below the line. As class zero. So let's take a look at this slide up. Which one of those combinations is actually going to give me AND gate? These are some kind of suggestions for the parameters, but obviously there are more than one correct solution and how we can actually come up with a different combination of That is your thought of one in tattoos to realize the end function. children.title: Deriving Equations for Neuron Outputs
children.summary: The segment discusses how to derive equations for a neuron with three unknowns: theta 0, theta 1, and theta 2. It explains that by setting specific input values (x1 = 1 and x2 = 0), one can create an equation to ensure the output is 0, which requires the weighted combination to be less than 0. The equation formulated is theta 0 + theta 1 * x1 + theta 2 * x2 < 0. Additionally, it introduces example parameters for an AND gate, illustrating how different values for theta can affect the output.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And why did I say that there is more than one correct solution here? So what I can actually do is that we have three unknowns. Right? So we have Teta 0, Teta 1 and Teta 2 that are unknowns. But I can actually write four equations in order to figure out, how can I actually come up with why values for all of these four rows that I have? Right? So I'm going to actually write down four equations there. So I'm assuming that, okay, if I go ahead and pass, say, x1 be equal to 1 and x2 equal to 0 into this neuron and then calculate the combination of those inputs that I pass, I need to make sure that the output is equal to 0. And I want to make sure that the output is equal to 0. This means that I need to be on this side of this step function. That weighted combination needs to be less than 0. So I will write this out. I'll say that, OK, I have theta 0 plus theta 1 times x1 in this case is equal to 1 plus theta 2 times x2, which in this case is equal to 0. This all should be less than 0 for me to be able to output a 0 value. because that's what my step function is actually going to do. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

But I can actually write four equations in order to figure out, how can I actually come up with why values for all of these four rows that I have? Right? So I'm going to actually write down four equations there. So I'm assuming that, okay, if I go ahead and pass, say, x1 be equal to 1 and x2 equal to 0 into this neuron and then calculate the combination of those inputs that I pass, I need to make sure that the output is equal to 0. And I want to make sure that the output is equal to 0. This means that I need to be on this side of this step function. That weighted combination needs to be less than 0. So I will write this out. I'll say that, OK, I have theta 0 plus theta 1 times x1 in this case is equal to 1 plus theta 2 times x2, which in this case is equal to 0. This all should be less than 0 for me to be able to output a 0 value. because that's what my step function is actually going to do.

children.title: Activation Function Conditions in Neural Networks
children.summary: The conditions for activation functions in neural networks are discussed, specifically focusing on the relationships between the parameters theta (θ). It is established that θ0 + θ1 should be less than zero, while θ0 + θ1 + θ2 should be greater than or equal to zero when the output is one. The speaker explains that for inputs resulting in an output of one, the activation must be non-negative, adhering to the step function's criteria. The discussion includes solving equations related to these conditions, emphasizing that certain parameter values, such as θ0, cannot equal zero based on the analysis of student responses.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So I'm going to simplify this, theta zero plus theta one should be less than zero. Kind of similarly, I can write that as theta zero plus theta one plus theta two should be greater than or equal to zero. Right? Because this is the case that why should be equal to one? So I should have an activation that's greater than or equal to zero. going to write the third line here that teta 0 plus teta 1 times x times 0 plus teta 2 times 0 which is all 0 should be less than 0. So that's the equation for the third kind of input and for this other one I'm going to have teta 0 plus teta 2 be less than 0. Bye. Yes. Because the question is that how am I getting greater than or equal to zero for the second line? This is because my output is equal to one. This is the only case that the output is equal to one and based on the step function, we said that if z is greater than or equal to zero, the output is equal to one and if z is less than zero, the output is equal to zero. So that's why for all of those other lines that I have zero, what I'm putting is, so these are all zeroes, so they all need to be less than zero, less than zero, less than zero except for the case that output is equal to one. Great question. Okay, so let me actually solve it to show you what's happening. So you can see from this line that your tet to zero could not have been equal to zero. children.title: Probabilistic View on Solution Spaces
children.summary: The discussion focuses on having four equations with three unknowns, illustrating the presence of multiple correct solutions. It highlights that while various lines can be drawn between two classes, only one line will maximize the loss function, which will be further explored by another speaker.

children.title: Probabilistic View on Solution Spaces
children.summary: The discussion centers on having four equations with three unknowns, highlighting the existence of multiple correct solutions. It emphasizes that although various lines can be drawn between two classes, only one line will maximize the loss function, which will be further explored by another speaker.

children.title: Probabilistic View on Solution Spaces
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the concept of having four equations with three unknowns, which indicates multiple correct solutions for the problem at hand. It emphasizes that while various lines can be drawn between two classes, only one line will maximize the loss function, a topic that will be explored further by another speaker.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So hypothetically, let's say that I go ahead and set that to zero equal to negative one. Right? And again, notice here that I have four equations, three unknowns. So that's the degree, that's the next degree of freedom that I have. That's why I have more than one correct solution here. So I can put multiple different lines between all of these, between these two classes. And all of those lines would be correct. But we're going to take a look at the probabilistic view of this and we'll see that only one of those lines is going to maximize the loss function that we have. And that's, I think what Joey is going to go over and show you what's going to happen. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Positive Values and Degrees of Freedom for Teta Variables
children.summary: The values of teta-0, teta-1, and teta-2 must be positive but not excessively so to satisfy specific summation conditions. An example shows teta-1 and teta-2 set to 0.5, fulfilling the equation that sums to zero. The concept of degrees of freedom is introduced, where the negative value of teta-0 serves as a constraint, while teta-1 and teta-2 are symmetric variables, resulting in a limiting equation.

children.title: Positive Values and Degrees of Freedom for Teta Variables
children.summary: The values of teta-0, teta-1, and teta-2 must be positive but not excessively so to meet specific summation conditions. An example is provided where teta-1 and teta-2 are both set to 0.5, which satisfies the equation summing to zero. The concept of degrees of freedom is introduced, explaining that the negative value of teta-0 acts as a constraint, while teta-1 and teta-2 are symmetric variables, leading to a limiting equation.

children.title: Positive Values for Teta Variables
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the requirement for the values of teta-0, teta-1, and teta-2 to be positive, but not excessively so, to ensure that their summation meets certain conditions. An example is given where teta-1 and teta-2 are both set to 0.5, demonstrating that this satisfies the equation where their summation equals zero. The concept of degrees of freedom is introduced, explaining how the negative value of teta-0 acts as a constraint, while teta-1 and teta-2 serve as symmetric variables, leading to a limiting equation.

Audio: So let's take a look at this case. So here in this case, I'm saying that, okay, the kind of another information that I'm getting here is that I need to have tet-0, tet-1 and tet-2 to be a positive value. So that's kind of a hint for me that tet-1 and tet-2 should be a positive value. But not so positive that only one of them by itself can push the summation of of teta 0 plus teta 2 to be a positive value. So I want something positive, but not so positive. So for example, here I would say that, OK, if teta 2 is equal to, for example, 0.5, and if also teta 1 is also, so I'm going to write it here, if teta 1 is also equal to 0.5, so I'm going to highlight all of these. for you, teta 1.5, teta 2.5, and then teta 1, negative 1. So you would be able to see that all of these equations are satisfied because then this big summation that was of concern here is going to be exactly equal to zero. Question. That's a valid point. I think the degrees of freedom in this case are coming from the case that We have equations that are that are simplifying tetah1 and tetat2 in some of the cases. Right? So for example, tetah0 being a negative value is a constraining kind of equation, right? But then what you would see is that then this is going to be a limiting equation right here, but then tetah1 and tetat2 are actually also acting as symmetric kind of variables. So in that sense, this equation right here, and this equation right here, are not two separate equations. because there's symmetric. Great. Good question. Alright, so that was kind of a warm-up. So for example, here I would say that, OK, if teta 2 is equal to, for example, 0.5, and if also teta 1 is also, so I'm going to write it here, if teta 1 is also equal to 0.5, so I'm going to highlight all of these. for you, teta 1.5, teta 2.5, and then teta 1, negative 1. I think the degrees of freedom in this case are coming from the case that We have equations that are that are simplifying tetah1 and tetat2 in some of the cases. Right? So for example, tetah0 being a negative value is a constraining kind of equation, right? But then what you would see is that then this is going to be a limiting equation right here, but then tetah1 and tetat2 are actually also acting as symmetric kind of variables. So in that sense, this equation right here, and this equation right here, are not two separate equations. because there's symmetric. Great. Good question. Alright, so that was kind of a warm-up.

children.title: Constructing Logic Gates and Digit Classification
children.summary: The segment discusses constructing OR and XOR gates using neurons, building upon the AND gate. It emphasizes experimenting with parameters theta_0, theta_1, and theta_2, providing examples for the AND gate to illustrate functional completeness in Boolean operations. For the OR gate, optimal parameters are identified, including a negative weight for theta_0 to prevent activation with zero inputs and positive weights for x1 and x2 for active outputs. The XOR gate is introduced, noting its output conditions and the requirement of at least three neurons for effective construction due to its complexity. The discussion highlights the need for combining classifiers with multiple neurons for accurate classification. Additionally, a handwritten digit classification example is presented, where images are represented by pixel values of 1 and 0, resized to 9 by 7 pixels, totaling 63 pixels. The serialization method organizes pixels for classification into 10 digit classes. In neural networks, output probabilities for digits are derived from 60 input pixels, with a specific neuron in the second layer showing strong connections to the first seven pixels, indicating its role in feature detection.

children.title: Constructing OR and XOR Gates with Neurons
children.summary: The segment covers the construction of an OR gate using neurons, building on the previously discussed AND gate, and encourages experimentation with parameters theta_0, theta_1, and theta_2. It provides examples of parameters for the AND gate, illustrating the functional completeness of Boolean operations. The discussion then shifts to finding optimal parameters for the OR gate, emphasizing the use of a negative weight for theta_0 to prevent activation when both inputs are zero, and the selection of weights for x1 and x2 to ensure a positive output when at least one input is active. The construction of an XOR gate is introduced, highlighting its output conditions and the challenge of classifying inputs, which differ from AND and OR gates. It is noted that a minimum of three neurons is needed to effectively build the XOR gate, as one neuron cannot capture the required complexity. Finally, the necessity of combining classifiers with multiple neurons for accurate classification is discussed.

children.title: Building an OR Gate with Neurons
children.summary: The segment discusses the construction of an OR gate using neurons, building on the previously established AND gate. It encourages experimentation to determine the optimal parameters for theta_0, theta_1, and theta_2 to successfully create the OR gate. Examples of parameters for the AND gate are provided, illustrating the functional completeness of both Boolean AND and OR operations.

children.title: Optimal Parameters for Neuron Activation
children.summary: The segment discusses finding optimal parameters for a neural network model, specifically for an OR gate. It emphasizes using a negative weight for the threshold (theta_0) to ensure the neuron does not activate when both inputs (x1 and x2) are zero. Additionally, it highlights the importance of selecting weights for x1 and x2 to ensure that if at least one input is activated, the summation results in a positive output. The instructor encourages students to experiment with different values for theta_1 and theta_2.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is another slide of question. Go ahead, spend few minutes on it. This is going to be a nice practice. You can use the same technique that I had. You can guess that is zero and then you can use kind of the same symmetric property between I'm going to touch the one and touch the two and then be able to find out what. uh... optimal parameters i need to have Yes, your answer should be in form of teta 0 equal to something that the one equal to something. I'm guessing that that answer might be correct. Okay, some negative, bioster, some positive. Oh! Values for PET-1 and PET-2. Let's go ahead and give this a try. So if I go ahead and say I think one suggestion was that Uh, that... One thing that I saw was that someone was suggesting that it was zero to be negative point four. This is because we just want to make sure that If I don't have activation on x1 and x2, if x1 and x2 are both equal to zero and no activation or no input, is coming through the input channels right there, I don't want the neuron to be activated. So that's why a negative weight for the tetra zero by itself is going to be very important. But at the same time, we want to make sure that we pick the weights for x1 and x2 So that if at least one of them is activated, this summation is actually positive, right? children.title: Building XOR Gate for Neural Networks
children.summary: The discussion focuses on constructing an XOR gate using a neural network. The XOR output is equal to 1 when only one of the two inputs is active, while it outputs 0 in all other cases. The challenge is highlighted in classifying inputs into classes zero and one, noting that their structure differs from that of AND and OR gates. The importance of finding optimum parameters for the neuron to achieve accurate classification is emphasized.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that when tet-1 is only, or the x-1 is only activated, and I sum it up with negative point four, it is greater than or equal to zero. So it can be 0.4 or above. So for example here if I pick 0.5 and here I pick 0.5 then I have all of the optimum values that I want in order to actually realize the or gate. So notice that again the task of creating a neuron is again for us to come up with the optimum parameters. And once we find the optimum parameters, then we have a function. And that function takes inputs and then generates the output perfectly and then it classifies points into classes of zeroes and months. But now let's take a look at x or gate. So for the x or gate, what's happening is that the x or value is going output is going to be equal to 1 in the cases that only one of the two is active. So again, if you're looking at the output is that only for this case and for this case, the output is equal to 1 and in other in all other cases, the output is equal to 0. So a question for you is that How can we build the X or Gate? Just brainstorm ideas. So I have an x or gate. I want to do a binary classification between class zero and class one. But now there is an issue that class zero and class one are kind of differently structured than what I saw in the AND gate and OR gate. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Building XOR Function with Neurons
children.summary: To create a classifier for the XOR function, it is necessary to have separate classifiers for each of the distinct classes represented by the green notes. A minimum of three neurons is required to effectively build this XOR gate, as one neuron alone is insufficient to capture the needed complexity.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Negative theta zero, changing activation function, combination of n and or gate. neurons, two functions. So that's kind of the idea. Again, I give you the hint on the plot that if I want to actually be able to build a classifier, I need to have a classifier for one of these green kind of notes separately and I need to have another classifier for the top green note separately in order to be able to separate out my classes, my data points to be be like the class one. So then the other question is that how many neurons do I need a minimum in order to find out, in order to build this x-ore gate? I'm definitely needing more than one. Who is closed, but not closed enough? or but there is a minimum. I need to have the minimum of three nodes in order or three neurons in order to build this function. children.title: Combining Classifiers with Neurons in Neural Networks
children.summary: To classify data using multiple linear classifiers, at least three neurons are required: one for each classifier and one to combine their outputs. Each neuron corresponds to a linear decision boundary, and the output is determined by taking the logical OR of the results from the individual classifiers. This setup allows for the separation of values in a truth table function, where if either classifier indicates class one, the overall output will also be class one. This illustrates the fundamental structure of neural networks and the limitations of individual neurons.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I need to have one classifier for one line. I need to have another classifier for the other line. And then I need to have a way of combining the two. So combining the two is this turd line right here that is actually going to actually need to be a classifier or another neuron that I need to build. So because I have tree linear kind of lines or linear classifiers here, I will need at least minimum tree neurons. So the way that it works is that, and the way, and why we need to actually have tree neurons is that if you actually try to separate out this truce table function using this chunk of normal form, so what's happening is that I can actually say that I'm separating this value of 1, which corresponds to this node right here in one truce table. So I'm going to build actually the classifier right here for this is specific truce table. And then I'm going to build another classifier that's going to actually give me that... Classifier for this other node, right? So this is another true staple and then what I need is actually taking the or between the two. So I would say that either you have to be classified into class one using the bottom classifier or you need to be classified into class one using the top classifier and then I'm going to take the or between the two and if you meet at least one of them then the output is going to be equal to one. So that's why you would see that this is one neuron. This right here is one neuron and then I would need to have another neuron in order to combine them and that's the minimum. And that's where the idea of neural network comes into play that there is the limitation that each neuron has because each neuron has a linear decision boundary. children.title: Combining Linear Decision Boundaries for Non-linear Classifiers
children.summary: To create non-linear decision boundaries, multiple linear decision boundaries can be combined. A demonstration is provided, showcasing the creation of AND, OR, and XOR gates as classifiers. The demo allows for adjustments in the number of samples and noise levels for the AND gate, illustrating how different gates can be implemented using combinations of functions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And what I need to do is if my decision boundary becomes non-linear, I need to combine multiple different linear decision boundaries together in order to build a non-linear decision boundary. So that's exactly what's happening. So let me quickly go through a demo and show you how this is actually going to happen. Let me restart this quickly. Okay, so let's take a look. So I'm here actually going to create a bunch of different gates. So I'm creating and gate or gate and x or gate to show you how it actually going to build classifiers for each one of these different gates that we have. The other thing that we do is actually showing you that we can come up with number of samples, we can pick the gate and then we can change the noise level. So for example, here if I go ahead and say that, I want AND gate with this specific noise level. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
[ ]:
# Function to generate a dataset with multiple samples per gate Location
def generate_data_with_noise(num_samples = 500, gate = "AND", noise_level = 0.05):
Generate multiple samples per logic gate configuration with added noise.
if gate == 'AND':
base_X = np.array([[0, 0], [0, 1], [1, 0], [1, 1]])
base_y = np.array([0, 0, 0, 1])
elif gate == 'OR':
base_X = np.array([[0, 0], [0, 1], [1, 0], [1, 1]])
base_y = np. array ([0, 1, 1, 1])
elif gate == 'XOR' :
base_X = np.array([[0, 0], [0, 1], [1, 0], [1, 1]])
base_y = np.array([0, 1, 1, 0])
else:
raise ValueError("Gate must be 'AND', 'OR', or 'XOR'.")
# Repeat each base configuration to create multiple samples
X = np.repeat(base_X, num_samples // len(base_X), axis=0)
y = np.repeat(base_y, num_samples // len(base_y), axis=0)
# Add Gaussian noise to the inputs
X = X + np.random.normal(0, noise_level, X.shape)
# Shuffle the dataset to avoid ordered samples
indices = np. arange(X. shape[0])
np. random. shuffle(indices)

# The following code defines a set of interactive widgets (sliders)
# and binds them to an update function that will be run whenever
a slider is changed.
@interact(num_samples=FloatSlider(min=100, max=1000, step=100, value=500, description='Samples'),
gate=Dropdown(options=['AND', 'OR', 'XOR' ], value='AND', description='Gate'),
noise_level=FloatSlider(min=0.0, max=1.0, step=0.01, value=0.05, description='Noise Level'))
def update_data_plot(num_samples, gate, noise_level):
X, y = generate_data_with_noise(num_samples, gate, noise_level)
with data_fig.batch_update():
data_fig.data[0].x = X[y == 0, 0]
data_fig.data[0].y = X[y == 0, 1]
data_fig.data[1].x = X[y == 1, 0]
data_fig.data[1].y = X[y == 1, 1]
data_fig.update_layout(title=f"Dataset for {gate} Gate with Noise Level {noise_level}")

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
[ ]:
# Function to generate a dataset with multiple samples per gate Location
def generate_data_with_noise(num_samples = 500, gate = "AND", noise_level = 0.05):
Generate multiple samples per logic gate configuration with added noise.
if gate == 'AND':
base_X = np.array([[0, 0], [0, 1], [1, 0], [1, 1]])
base_y = np.array([0, 0, 0, 1])
elif gate == 'OR':
base_X = np.array([[0, 0], [0, 1], [1, 0], [1, 1]])
base_y = np. array ([0, 1, 1, 1])
elif gate == 'XOR' :
base_X = np.array([[0, 0], [0, 1], [1, 0], [1, 1]])
base_y = np.array([0, 1, 1, 0])
else:
raise ValueError("Gate must be 'AND', 'OR', or 'XOR'.")
# Repeat each base configuration to create multiple samples
X = np.repeat(base_X, num_samples // len(base_X), axis=0)
y = np.repeat(base_y, num_samples // len(base_y), axis=0)
# Add Gaussian noise to the inputs
X = X + np.random.normal(0, noise_level, X.shape)
# Shuffle the dataset to avoid ordered samples
indices = np. arange(X. shape[0])
np. random. shuffle(indices)

@interact(num_samples=FloatSlider(min=100, max=1000, step=100, value=500, description='Samples'),
gate=Dropdown(options=['AND', 'OR', 'XOR' ], value='AND', description='Gate'),

def update_data_plot(num_samples, gate, noise_level):
X, y = generate_data_with_noise(num_samples, gate, noise_level)
with data_fig.batch_update():
data_fig.data[0].x = X[y == 0, 0]
data_fig.data[0].y = X[y == 0, 1]
data_fig.data[1].x = X[y == 1, 0]
data_fig.data[1].y = X[y == 1, 1]
data_fig.update_layout(title=f"Dataset for {gate} Gate with Noise Level {noise_level}")

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
[ ]:
# Function to generate a dataset with multiple samples per gate Location
def generate_data_with_noise(num_samples = 500, gate = "AND", noise_level = 0.05):
Generate multiple samples per logic gate configuration with added noise.
if gate == 'AND':
base_X = np.array([[0, 0], [0, 1], [1, 0], [1, 1]])
base_y = np.array([0, 0, 0, 1])
elif gate == 'OR':
base_X = np.array([[0, 0], [0, 1], [1, 0], [1, 1]])
base_y = np. array ([0, 1, 1, 1])
elif gate == 'XOR' :
base_X = np.array([[0, 0], [0, 1], [1, 0], [1, 1]])
base_y = np.array([0, 1, 1, 0])
else:
raise ValueError("Gate must be 'AND', 'OR', or 'XOR'.")
# Repeat each base configuration to create multiple samples
X = np.repeat(base_X, num_samples // len(base_X), axis=0)
y = np.repeat(base_y, num_samples // len(base_y), axis=0)
# Add Gaussian noise to the inputs
X = X + np.random.normal(0, noise_level, X.shape)
# Shuffle the dataset to avoid ordered samples
indices = np. arange(X. shape[0])
np. random. shuffle(indices)

children.title: Building Classifiers for AND and OR Gates
children.summary: A logistic regression classifier is developed for an AND gate using scikit-learn, where data points are classified based on a logistic activation function. If the probability exceeds 50%, the point is classified as class one; otherwise, it is class zero. The training and test errors are evaluated, showing 100% accuracy for the AND gate classification. The process is similar for the OR gate, indicating the classifier can be retrained to adapt to different logical operations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So you would see that I only have one class of one. So this right here belongs to the data points to belong to class of one, and every red dot belongs to class of zero. So what I want to do is I want to build a classifier for this AND gate. So let's take a look at how we can actually do this with just a simple logistic regression. His logistic regression with the F-stein was that... It logistics function by itself with a neuron so it can just assume that if you're calculating a weighted combination of inputs we're passing it to the logistic activation the logistic activation is going to give me probability again if the probability is above 50% I've classified into class one if it is below the 50% I classified as class zero. So this is kind of performing logistic regression using scikit-learn, splitting the data as usual, creating a logistic function, fitting it to our training data. And then what we would do is we'll take a look at the training and the test error that we have. So, dysfunction plus decision boundary beyond the scope for data 100, but let's take a look at the output. So you see that now my output is actually showing me that we were able to classify with 100% accuracy between the two classes on the AND gate. So this is kind of visualizing exactly what you'd seen in bit logistic regression before. So if I change the gate to OR gate. You would see that now it retrains again. Logistic Regression Using Scikit-learn
This section demonstrates how to perform logistic regression using the scikit-learn library. The dataset is divided into training and testing subsets
using an 80%-20% ratio with the train_test_split function. A logistic regression model is instantiated and trained on the training dataset using the
. fit() method. The model's performance is evaluated on both the training and testing data using the . score() method, which computes
accuracy.

#
Train a logistic regression model

test_error = model.score(x_test, y_test)

Logistic Regression Using Scikit-learn
This section demonstrates how to perform logistic regression using the scikit-learn library. The dataset is divided into training and testing subsets
using an 80%-20% ratio with the train_test_split function. A logistic regression model is instantiated and trained on the training dataset using the
. fit() method. The model's performance is evaluated on both the training and testing data using the . score() method, which computes
accuracy.
[ ]:
from sklearn. linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
# Logistic Regression Using Scikit-Learn
def perform_logistic_regression(X, y):
# Split the data
I
x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=140)
# Train a logistic regression model
model = LogisticRegression() .fit(x_train, y_train)
# Print training and testing scores
train_error = model.score(x_train, y_train)
test_error = model.score(x_test, y_test)
return model, train_error, test_error

def plot_decision_boundary(model, xrange, yrange, num_points=100, probs=True):
# Generate a grid of points
xx, yy = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(xrange[0], xrange[1], num_points),

# Get predictions for the grid
if probs:
preds = model.predict_proba(grid) [ :, 1]. reshape(xx. shape)
else:
preds = model.predict(grid) .reshape(xx. shape)
return go. Contour(x=xx[0], y=yy[ : , 0], z=preds, colorscale=[[0, 'red'], [1, 'blue']],

This section demonstrates how to perform logistic regression using the scikit-learn library. The dataset is divided into training and testing subsets
using an 80%-20% ratio with the train_test_split function. A logistic regression model is instantiated and trained on the training dataset using the
. fit() method. The model's performance is evaluated on both the training and testing data using the . score() method, which computes
accuracy.

def plot_decision_boundary(model, xrange, yrange, num_points=100, probs=True):
# Generate a grid of points
xx, yy = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(xrange[0], xrange[1], num_points),
np. linspace(yrange[0], yrange[1], num_points))
grid = np.c[xx.ravel(), yy.ravel()]
# Get predictions for the grid
if probs:
preds = model.predict_proba(grid) [ :, 1]. reshape(xx. shape)
else:
preds = model.predict(grid) .reshape(xx. shape)
return go. Contour(x=xx[0], y=yy[ : , 0], z=preds, colorscale=[[0, 'red'], [1, 'blue']],
opacity = 0.5, showscale=False)

children.title: Logistic Regression and Decision Boundary Explanation
children.summary: The logistic function is used to find the decision boundary with parameters theta 0, theta 1, and theta 2. As data points move along the x-axis, the model transitions from classifying points as class 0 to class 1, demonstrating varying probabilities. The logistic regression aims to find the decision boundary where z equals 0, maximizing the likelihood of correctly classifying data points. This concept will be revisited in the context of neural networks using PyTorch, which is an industry-standard tool for building neural networks.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It finds the decision boundary using the logistic function. Again, the logistic function is going to have teta 0, teta 1 and teta 2. And the line is actually the equation for the optimum set of parameters for the logistic function. If I go ahead and show you the probabilities, you would see that in the far left part, the model is confident that any data point that would be here is going to be classified as class 0. And then as we move here, it is going to have lower probability for class 0 and then getting higher probability for class 1. And then as we move to the blue regions, then it's going to be more confident into classifying the data point as class 1. So what the logistic regression is actually doing is that it finds the decision boundary of z equal to 0 that cuts between the two kind of sets of data points such that the kind of a combination of those probabilities is maximized. But it's not talking about the notion of likelihood, but it tries to just generally look at the likelihood of all of these points that are on the plot and tries to maximize or put the points in the regions that are corresponding to each month of them. So the next thing that I want to show you, and we're going to actually revisit this in the neural network part of today's lecture is PyTorch. So we could actually do exactly the same thing that we did with logistic aggression using scikit-learn using PyTorch. And PyTorch is kind of an industry standard for using and building neural networks. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

PyTorch
We can now try to repeat the modeling process using PyTorch. PyTorch is a popular deep learning library that is widely used in the research
community. It is a lower-level library than scikit-learn and requires more code to accomplish the same tasks. However, it is more flexible and can be
used to build more complex models.
[ ]:
# PyTorch
import torch
# Neural Network Class in pyTorch
import torch.nn as nn
# Optimizer Library in pyTorch (for SGD)
import torch.optim as optim
Step 0: Working with Data in Pytorch
At the core of PyTorch is the torch. Tensor class. This class is similar to numpy arrays but with some additional features. PyTorch tensors can be
used to store data and perform operations on that data. Pytorch tensors can also be used to store gradients, which are used to update the parameters
of a model during training.
To use PyTorch, we need to convert our data to tensors. We can do this using the torch. tensor function, or we can use the torch. from_numpy
function to convert numpy arrays to tensors.
Notice that tensors are converted to type float 32 . This is because PyTorch is built around float32 instead of float64 (the standard format for
numpy ). This is because most of the math done on GPUs is in lower precision. The labels ( y_train ) are reshaped using . unsqueeze(1) , which
adds an additional dimension to match the model's expected output shape.

PyTorch
We can now try to repeat the modeling process using PyTorch. PyTorch is a popular deep learning library that is widely used in the research
community. It is a lower-level library than scikit-learn and requires more code to accomplish the same tasks. However, it is more flexible and can be
used to build more complex models.
[ ]:
# PyTorch
import torch
# Neural Network Class in pyTorch
import torch.nn as nn
# Optimizer Library in pyTorch (for SGD)
import torch.optim as optim
Step 0: Working with Data in Pytorch
At the core of PyTorch is the torch. Tensor class. This class is similar to numpy arrays but with some additional features. PyTorch tensors can be
used to store data and perform operations on that data. Pytorch tensors can also be used to store gradients, which are used to update the parameters
of a model during training.
To use PyTorch, we need to convert our data to tensors. We can do this using the torch. tensor function, or we can use the torch. from_numpy
function to convert numpy arrays to tensors.
Notice that tensors are converted to type float 32 . This is because PyTorch is built around float32 instead of float64 (the standard format for
numpy ). This is because most of the math done on GPUs is in lower precision. The labels ( y_train ) are reshaped using . unsqueeze(1) , which
adds an additional dimension to match the model's expected output shape.

PyTorch
We can now try to repeat the modeling process using PyTorch. PyTorch is a popular deep learning library that is widely used in the research
community. It is a lower-level library than scikit-learn and requires more code to accomplish the same tasks. However, it is more flexible and can be
used to build more complex models.
[ ]:
# PyTorch
import torch
# Neural Network Class in pyTorch
import torch.nn as nn
# Optimizer Library in pyTorch (for SGD)
import torch.optim as optim
Step 0: Working with Data in Pytorch
At the core of PyTorch is the torch. Tensor class. This class is similar to numpy arrays but with some additional features. PyTorch tensors can be
used to store data and perform operations on that data. Pytorch tensors can also be used to store gradients, which are used to update the parameters
of a model during training.
To use PyTorch, we need to convert our data to tensors. We can do this using the torch. tensor function, or we can use the torch. from_numpy
function to convert numpy arrays to tensors.
Notice that tensors are converted to type float32 . This is because PyTorch is built around float32 instead of float64 (the standard format for
numpy ). This is because most of the math done on GPUs is in lower precision. The labels ( y_train ) are reshaped using . unsqueeze(1) , which
adds an additional dimension to match the model's expected output shape.

PyTorch
We can now try to repeat the modeling process using PyTorch. PyTorch is a popular deep learning library that is widely used in the research
community. It is a lower-level library than scikit-learn and requires more code to accomplish the same tasks. However, it is more flexible and can be
used to build more complex models.
[ ]:
# PyTorch
import torch
# Neural Network Class in pyTorch
import torch.nn as nn
# Optimizer Library in pyTorch (for SGD)
import torch.optim as optim
Step 0: Working with Data in Pytorch
At the core of PyTorch is the torch. Tensor class. This class is similar to numpy arrays but with some additional features. PyTorch tensors can be
used to store data and perform operations on that data. Pytorch tensors can also be used to store gradients, which are used to update the parameters
of a model during training.
To use PyTorch, we need to convert our data to tensors. We can do this using the torch. tensor function, or we can use the torch. from_numpy
function to convert numpy arrays to tensors.
Notice that tensors are converted to type float 32 . This is because PyTorch is built around float32 instead of float64 (the standard format for
numpy ). This is because most of the math done on GPUs is in lower precision. The labels ( y_train ) are reshaped using . unsqueeze(1) , which
adds an additional dimension to match the model's expected output shape.
