used to store data and perform operations on that data. Pytorch tensors can also be used to store gradients, which are used to update the parameters
of a model during training.
To use PyTorch, we need to convert our data to tensors. We can do this using the torch. tensor function, or we can use the torch. from_numpy
function to convert numpy arrays to tensors.
Notice that tensors are converted to type float 32 . This is because PyTorch is built around float32 instead of float64 (the standard format for
numpy ). This is because most of the math done on GPUs is in lower precision. The labels ( y_train ) are reshaped using . unsqueeze(1) , which
adds an additional dimension to match the model's expected output shape.

children.title: Using PyTorch for Neural Network Modeling
children.summary: PyTorch is introduced as a deep learning library that offers more control but requires more code compared to higher-level libraries like scikit-learn. The process of preparing datasets for PyTorch involves transforming data into tensors, which are similar to Numpy arrays and are used for storing data and model weights. A tensor dataset is created by converting the x and y datasets into tensors, followed by performing a train-test split using PyTorch's random split function.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's a little bit more complicated to use. You need to write a lot of code in order to do exactly the same thing, but it gives you a lot of control. So here what I'm doing is I'm importing Torch and I'm also importing some of the different utilities that I want to use. So for example here I would say that I want to actually use the neural network class, I want to have the optimizer from PyTorch and so on and so forth. So now we're going through exactly the same process that we had in building any kind of model that we had in data 100. So specifically here we would say that I need to prepare my data set. So the way that we use data sets or prepare data sets for pytorges to use is that we need to transform our data into tensors. Tensors is kind of similar to Numpy. It's also another kind of data structure that pytorges uses. It is helping in terms of storing the data. It is also helping in terms of storing the weights for the models that we're training and also updating those weights on the tensors as well. So let's take a look at what we do. We're creating a tensor dataset. It's a combination of my x that is transformed into a tensor. So, torshtot tensor transforms the dataset that I have, the x dataset that I have into tensors. It does exactly the same thing for y. And then what we do is actually do the train test split or random split using random split function from PyTorch. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

return train_data, test_data

We can now try to repeat the modeling process using PyTorch. PyTorch is a popular deep learning library that is widely used in the research
community. It is a lower-level library than scikit-learn and requires more code to accomplish the same tasks. However, it is more flexible and can be
used to build more complex models.
[ ]:
# PyTorch
import torch
# Neural Network Class in pyTorch
import torch.nn as nn
# Optimizer Library in pyTorch (for SGD)
import torch.optim as optim
Step 0: Working with Data in Pytorch
At the core of PyTorch is the torch. Tensor class. This class is similar to numpy arrays but with some additional features. PyTorch tensors can be
used to store data and perform operations on that data. Pytorch tensors can also be used to store gradients, which are used to update the parameters
of a model during training.
To use PyTorch, we need to convert our data to tensors. We can do this using the torch. tensor function, or we can use the torch. from_numpy
function to convert numpy arrays to tensors.
Notice that tensors are converted to type float32 . This is because PyTorch is built around float32 instead of float64 (the standard format for
numpy ). This is because most of the math done on GPUs is in lower precision. The labels ( y_train ) are reshaped using . unsqueeze(1) , which
adds an additional dimension to match the model's expected output shape.

children.title: Training/Test Split and Model Definition in PyTorch
children.summary: The training/test split can be efficiently done using multi-way splits, allowing for simultaneous division into training, validation, and test sets. In this example, 80% of the data is used for training and 20% for testing. To define a model in PyTorch, a class must be created, which specifies the neuron structure and activation functions. Data must be converted to tensors using the appropriate PyTorch functions, ensuring that they are in float32 format, as this is the standard for GPU computations. The labels are reshaped to match the model's output requirements. The logistic regression model is defined using a class that inherits from nn.Module, which is crucial for managing layers and parameters, and includes a single linear layer defined with nn.Linear.

To use PyTorch, we need to convert our data to tensors. We can do this using the torc
*
T
function to convert numpy arrays to tensors.
Notice that tensors are converted to type float32 . This is because PyTorch is built around float32 instead of float64 (t
numpy ). This is because most of the math done on GPUs is in lower precision. The labels ( y_train ) are reshaped using . un
adds an additional dimension to match the model's expected output shape.

def make_tensors(X, y):
from torch. utils.data import random_split, TensorDataset
data = TensorDataset(torch.tensor(X, dtype=torch.float32),
torch. tensor(y, dtype=torch.float32). unsqueeze(1))
torch.manual_seed (140)
train_data, test_data = random_split(data, [0.8, 0.2])
return train_data, test_data
Step 1: Defining the Logistic Regression Model
The logistic regression model is implemented in PyTorch using the LogisticRegressionModel class, which inherits from nn
inheriting from nn. Module is essential, as it provides the necessary methods to manage layers and parameters within the mo
method, a single linear layer is defined using nn. Linear(input_size, 1) . This layer computes a weighted sum of the input
term to form the mathematical basis of logistic regression. The input_size specifies the number of features in the dataset. In
method, the model performs the forward pass by applying the linear transformation followed by the sigmoid activation functio
The sigmoid activation ensures that the output values are in the range [0,1] so that the output is suitable for binary classificatio

children.title: Initialization of Logistic Regression Model in PyTorch
children.summary: A custom class called Logistic Regression A is created, inheriting from PyTorch's nn.Module. During initialization, the model's intercept is set to 1, and all weights are initialized to tensors of ones. It is noted that initializing weights this way is not effective for neural networks, prompting the need for alternative initialization methods. The model then computes a weighted combination of inputs, represented as the sum of the intercept and the product of weights and inputs. The activation is calculated, followed by applying the sigmoid function to obtain the output. This foundational code will be useful for building a neural network.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And also, kind of setting up the initialization for where you want to the tetasero-theta-1-theta-2 to be initialized. So here I'm creating this custom class. It's called Logistic Regression A. It is inheriting from NN.modules. So this is a neural network class from PyTorch that has all of the functionalities for you to deal with different models. So in the initialization function, what I'm doing is I'm just initializing. I'm saying that set the intercept of the model to be initialized with the value of 1 and set also the parameter for the model to be also initialized with a tensor of all one values. So initializing all of the weights with the value of one, we'll see that that's actually not working with neural networks. So we have to have a different creative way of designing a neural network, or initializing a neural network. So what we also do is we would say that now that you have all of those weights, you have intercept, you have W, go ahead and calculate a weighted combination of inputs. So here is tet0 plus tet1 times, or tet0 plus the summation of teti times xi. So we're getting the activation, then we're actually calculating sigmoid of that activation, and sigmoid of activation is going to be the output that you want. So it's a lot of code for doing exactly the same thing, but it will be useful when we're actually building a neural network. So right now I'm building a neural network only with one new rock. children.title: Logistic Regression Model Implementation
children.summary: A linear layer is defined to calculate the output as the sigmoid of a linear combination of inputs and weights. The logistic regression model is instantiated with two inputs, and the binary cross-entropy loss function is specified. The training process involves creating a data loader for batching, initializing a stochastic gradient descent optimizer, and setting the learning rate to 0.5 to update model parameters.

Step 3: Optimize the Loss
The optimizer used is stochastic gradient descent ( optim. SGD ). The optimizer updates the model's parameters during training to minimiz
takes the model's parameters and the learning rate as inputs. We could actually try more advanced optimizers like Adam. Try uncommentin
optimizer line.
The training process in PyTorch is handled within a loop that iterates over the dataset multiple epochs. For each epoch, we iterate over the
data in batches. For each batch, the following steps are performed:
The gradients from the previous iteration are cleared using optimizer . zero_grad() .
The forward pass is executed by passing the training data through the model, which computes predictions.
The predictions are compared with the true labels using the loss function, and the loss is calculated.
Backpropagation is performed using loss . backward () , which computes the gradients of the loss with respect to the model's param
Finally, the optimizer updates the model's parameters using these gradients through the optimizer . step() method.

def perform_logistic_regression_pytorch(train_dataset,
test_dataset,
model, loss_fn,
batch_size=64,
nepochs=20) :
from torch. utils.data import DataLoader
# Create a dataloader for training
train_loader - DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True)
test_loader = DataLoader(test_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=False)
# Define the optimizer (this is the update rule)
optimizer = optim. SGD(model.parameters(), 1r=0.5)
# optimizer = optim. Adam(model.parameters(), Lr=0.5)
for epoch in range (nepochs):
# Loop through all the batches
for batch, (X, y) in enumerate(train_loader):
# Zero the gradients to start the next step
optimizer.zero_grad()
# Compute prediction and Loss
pred = model (X)
loss = loss_fn(pred, y)
# Backpropagation (compute the gradient)
loss. backward()
# Update the parameters using the optimizer's update rule
optimizer.step()

Step 3: Optimize the Loss
The optimizer used is stochastic gradient descent ( optim. SGD ). The optimizer updates the model's parameters during training to minimiz
takes the model's parameters and the learning rate as inputs. We could actually try more advanced optimizers like Adam. Try uncommentin
optimizer line.
The training process in PyTorch is handled within a loop that iterates over the dataset multiple epochs. For each epoch, we iterate over the
data in batches. For each batch, the following steps are performed:
The gradients from the previous iteration are cleared using optimizer . zero_grad() .
The forward pass is executed by passing the training data through the model, which computes predictions.
The predictions are compared with the true labels using the loss function, and the loss is calculated.
Backpropagation is performed using loss . backward () , which computes the gradients of the loss with respect to the model's param
Finally, the optimizer updates the model's parameters using these gradients through the optimizer . step() method.

def perform_logistic_regression_pytorch(train_dataset,
test_dataset,
model, loss_fn,
batch_size=64,
nepochs=20) :
from torch. utils.data import DataLoader
# Create a dataloader for training
train_loader - DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True)
test_loader = DataLoader(test_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=False)
# Define the optimizer (this is the update rule)
optimizer = optim. SGD(model.parameters(), 1r=0.5)
# optimizer = optim. Adam(model.parameters(), Lr=0.5)
for epoch in range (nepochs):
# Loop through all the batches
for batch, (X, y) in enumerate(train_loader):
# Zero the gradients to start the next step
optimizer.zero_grad()
# Compute prediction and Loss
pred = model (X)
loss = loss_fn(pred, y)
# Backpropagation (compute the gradient)
loss. backward()
# Update the parameters using the optimizer's update rule
optimizer.step()

def perform_logistic_regression_pytorch(train_dataset,
test_dataset,
model, loss_fn,
batch_size=64,
nepochs=20) :
from torch. utils.data import DataLoader
# Create a dataloader for training
train_loader - DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True)
test_loader = DataLoader(test_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=False)
# Define the optimizer (this is the update rule)
optimizer = optim. SGD(model.parameters(), 1r=0.5)
# optimizer = optim. Adam(model.parameters(), Lr=0.5)
for epoch in range (nepochs):
# Loop through all the batches
for batch, (X, y) in enumerate(train_loader):
# Zero the gradients to start the next step
optimizer.zero_grad()
# Compute prediction and Loss
pred = model (X)
loss = loss_fn(pred, y)
# Backpropagation (compute the gradient)
loss. backward()
# Update the parameters using the optimizer's update rule
optimizer.step()

children.title: Logistic Regression Implementation in PyTorch
children.summary: The segment outlines the process of performing logistic regression using PyTorch. It describes the creation of data loaders for training and testing datasets, defines the optimizer with stochastic gradient descent (SGD), and details the training loop over specified epochs. In each epoch, it zeroes the gradients, computes predictions and loss, performs backpropagation to calculate gradients, and updates the model parameters using the optimizer's update rule.

children.title: Training Neural Networks with Epochs and Loss Visualization
children.summary: The segment discusses running a training session for a neural network, highlighting the training and test errors across different epochs. It emphasizes the impact of noise on the model's performance, illustrating how the loss decreases from random initialization but remains unstable due to the noisy model. The demonstration includes changing the model to an OR gate and observing the loss trend. However, when applying the XOR function, it is noted that the decision boundary is not optimal, as the data is not linearly separable, resulting in high loss that the model struggles to optimize.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we're gonna actually run it. and then see how it's actually going to do the training. So different epochs what we have the training error for each one of those epochs and the test error that we'd seen in each one of those epochs. And if I go ahead and visualize what's actually happening. You would be able to see that. Okay, what's happening is that it went ahead created all of those did the update through all of those iterations and then at the end it's not giving me this decision boundary that we have. So if I go ahead and for example change this to an or a gate and I increase the noise level you would actually see that the model is actually training through multiple different epochs who would see that the loss is starts from some random initialization or some random loss and then it will keep reducing and so on and so forth. And what you're actually seeing is that the loss is not as stable because my model is noisy. In different batches with different configurations it's actually going to do, is going to have a different loss and overall it tries to trend down and reduce the loss for the training data. So that was a quick demo on what's happening with these neurons. But what I didn't show you was that with all of these beautiful things that we had with and then, or and the nice decision boundary, I don't get that much of a nice decision boundary for X or. Because if you take a look at the X or, it tries to just put a line and this line actually is not going to be very optimum because the data is not linearly separable. And you would see that in the training, what's happening is that it's kind of hopeless. It sticks at a very high loss and it can't optimize the loss. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

And if I go ahead and visualize what's actually happening. You would be able to see that. Okay, what's happening is that it went ahead created all of those did the update through all of those iterations and then at the end it's not giving me this decision boundary that we have. So if I go ahead and for example change this to an or a gate and I increase the noise level you would actually see that the model is actually training through multiple different epochs who would see that the loss is starts from some random initialization or some random loss and then it will keep reducing and so on and so forth. And what you're actually seeing is that the loss is not as stable because my model is noisy. In different batches with different configurations it's actually going to do, is going to have a different loss and overall it tries to trend down and reduce the loss for the training data. So that was a quick demo on what's happening with these neurons. But what I didn't show you was that with all of these beautiful things that we had with and then, or and the nice decision boundary, I don't get that much of a nice decision boundary for X or. Because if you take a look at the X or, it tries to just put a line and this line actually is not going to be very optimum because the data is not linearly separable. And you would see that in the training, what's happening is that it's kind of hopeless. It sticks at a very high loss and it can't optimize the loss.

# Create a dataloader for training

test_loader = DataLoader(test_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=False)
# Define the optimizer (this is the update rule)
optimizer = optim. SGD(model.parameters(), 1r=0.5)
# optimizer = optim. Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.5)
for epoch in range(nepochs):
# Loop through all the batches
for batch, (X, y) in enumerate(train_loader):
# Zero the gradients to start the next step
optimizer.zero_grad()
# Compute prediction and Loss
pred = model (X)
loss = loss_fn(pred, y)
# Backpropagation (compute the gradient)
loss.backward()
# Update the parameters using the optimizer's update rule
optimizer.step()

children.title: Model Complexity and Loss Optimization
children.summary: A discussion on model complexity highlights that if a model is not complex enough, it fails to learn desired patterns, resulting in no optimization of loss. An example using an end gate illustrates how loss can be reduced from 0.6 to 0.4, while an XOR gate struggles to lower loss, remaining around 0.6 to 0.7.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: That's kind of the way that you would know that your model is not complex enough to learn the patterns that you want. So nothing is actually being optimized in the same way that we saw things being optimized or lost being reduced, for example, when I had the end gate. So take a look at the end gate. It tries to generally reduce the loss from 0.6 to 0.4 and then so on and so forth, but with the x or that's actually not. possible and the sticks around 0.7 or high 0.6 values. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X
lec27 (2) - JupyterLab
X

children.title: Introduction to Neural Networks and Function Optimization
children.summary: Neural networks consist of interconnected neurons that process inputs and produce outputs, similar to brain function. Each neuron receives inputs from multiple sources, applies an activation function, and passes the output to other neurons. The configuration of these connections determines the network's function, with each connection having associated weights. During training, these weights are updated to optimize the network's performance in classifying data points or performing specific tasks.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I'm going to quickly show you neural network. Why do we need to use this idea of neural network and what's different in neural network? So effectively, what's happening is that I said that we need to come by multiple neurons. The neurons are going to read input from each other and then they're going to do the processing and then calculating the output. This was a simple neuron, multiple inputs, activation sigmoid, we played with it for end or an x or gate that we had. The inputs can actually come from different neurons, It's kind of in the same way that in a brain, different neurons are connected to each other and they do the processing, they pass the output and then the next neuron is going to receive it. So we'll see that we can have multiple neurons kind of connected to it together into kind of a neural network, a structure that we have. So the configuration that we decide for how the neurons are connected together is going to give us a different function. What's happening is that all of these connections are having different weights. And what we do in the training is that we go ahead and do one update on all of those weights in order to make sure that our function is optimized and our weights. Or we find a combination of weights that is accurately classifying the data points or doing the tasks that we actually want to do. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Fully Connected Feed-Forward Network Overview
children.summary: A fully connected feed-forward network has all nodes in one layer connected to every node in the subsequent layer, allowing for comprehensive information flow. Each connection has associated weights and a bias term. Training the network involves optimizing these parameters to enhance performance. An example is given where input values are specified, illustrating how connections and weights function in the network.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So when we say fully connected feed-forward network, that means that all the connections are being made from back, from the previous layer to the next layer, and then all the nodes are connected to each other. So for example, here you would see that I have one node right here, and then this node is connected to all of the neurons in the next layer. So you would see that there is connection between this neuron and both of the neurons in the next layer. So fully connected means that everything is fully connected, Feet forward means that the information is being fed forward from the first layer to the next layer. So what you're seeing here is that I have inputs. I have, for example, weights on each one of these connections. So the value of one is the bias term and then I have weights on all of those connections. And hypothetically, if I go ahead and say that I'm inputting the value of one and negative one. So assume that this is my x-fun and assume that this is my x-2. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Feed Forward Neural Network Calculation
children.summary: In a feed forward neural network, the inputs are used to calculate the incoming Z value to a neuron, which involves multiplying the inputs by their respective weights and adding a bias term. For example, an incoming Z value of four results from calculations involving weights and biases, leading to an activation function output of 0.98, indicating high activation. The connection weights between neurons determine whether the next neuron will activate or deactivate; positive weights amplify excitation while negative weights inhibit activation. This process is repeated for subsequent layers, where the outputs from the first layer (0.98 and 0.12) are fed into the second layer for further calculations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What's happening in a feed forward neural network is that those inputs are going to actually Calculate the incoming Z to the neuron. And the incoming z to this neuron is going to be 1 multiplied to 1. and the negative one multiplied to negative two and then adding the bias term so this is going to give me activation or the input activation of Four. So what's happening with that input activation of four is that this is my z equal to four is going to be passed to the activation function. The activation function is going to decide that the output is going to be point nine eight. So that's a high activation. This neuron is active. Then depending on the weight between this neuron and for example this other neuron that we have here, whether this weight is positive or this weight is negative. Then the next neuron is going to decide whether it uses that input in order to activate itself or deactivate itself. Because positive connections are going to amplify excitation and the negative base are going to try to inhibit the neuron from activating. Because then it draws the activation to be closer to zero. So I'll do exactly the same thing, but the other node is equal to negative two. The output is going to be 0.12. And then now I have the inputs available at the second layer. So the inputs of the second layer are 0.98.12. Then they can be fed forward and we can really calculate the output from each one of the layers before the output is going to the next layer. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Neural Network as a Complex Function
children.summary: A neural network operates as a function, similar to those used in linear and logistic regression, but is more complex due to multiple interconnected parameters. When different input vectors are provided, such as (1, -1) or (0, 0), the network produces corresponding output vectors, demonstrating its functional nature. The structure of a neural network allows it to learn and represent more complex functions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So sequentially all of those values are going to be calculated. So what you notice is that when my input was one and negative one, my output is 0.62 and 0.83. If I change the input to 0 and 0, I'll get a different output. So effectively what's happening is that this neural network is a function. Remember that we had a function for linear regression. We had a function for a logistic regression. Neural network is also a function. It's just a very complicated function, but multiple different parameters connected to each other so that we can actually learn a more complex function. So this is a function input vector going in, output vector going out. For example, for the vector of 1 and negative 1, I got this output for another input. I'm going to get another output. But the bottom line is that a neural network is structured that you build is going to give you a function set. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X
lec27 (2) - JupyterLab

leaf_chunk.audio: So sequentially all of those values are going to be calculated. So what you notice is that when my input was one and negative one, my output is 0.62 and 0.83. If I change the input to 0 and 0, I'll get a different output. So effectively what's happening is that this neural network is a function. Remember that we had a function for linear regression. We had a function for a logistic regression. Neural network is also a function. It's just a very complicated function, but multiple different parameters connected to each other so that we can actually learn a more complex function. So this is a function input vector going in, output vector going out. For example, for the vector of 1 and negative 1, I got this output for another input. I'm going to get another output. But the bottom line is that a neural network is structured that you build is going to give you a function set.

children.title: Neural Network Architecture and Function Sets
children.summary: Functions in a neural network are determined by the combination of theta values, with each unique set of parameters yielding a different function. The goal of training is to identify the optimal function that maximizes accuracy or minimizes loss. Neural networks consist of an input layer, multiple hidden layers, and an output layer. The number of neurons in each layer and the total number of layers can be customized, with fully connected networks linking all neurons in adjacent layers. Deep neural networks, characterized by multiple hidden layers, are capable of learning more complex features as additional layers and neurons are added.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Why am I saying functions set? Because for every combination of all combinations of theta values that you have on the network, you'll have a different function. You change the parameters, you'll have a different function. That's why the architecture itself is going to give you a function set that what we're trying to do through the training is that find the optimum function in the function set, meaning that find the optimum set of parameters in the entire network that does the job with the higher accuracy or with the lower loss that we want. So I'm going to show you some terminology generally in neural network, we would say that we have an input layer, input goes in, we have multiple layers so we can actually stack layers on top of each other and we also have different neurons or different sizes in each one of the layers. I can say that okay, layer 2 is going to have 10 neurons or layer 2 is going to have 100 neurons and then I can actually build this structure however I want. one. I can decide on the number of layers. I can decide on the number of neurons in each one of the layers. If it is a fully connected network, all the neurons are going to be connected to the next layer neurons. And then at the end, I'm going to also have the output layer. So what's happening is that I have input layer, I have output layer and anything in between is called hidden layer. When you're talking about deep neural network, that means that a neural network that has multiple hidden layers. So that's just simple as that. Why is having multiple hidden layers important? It's because the neural network is going to get more complex, it's going to learn higher complexity features. So I'm going to talk to you about why we keep adding neurons and why did I say that as we add number of number of layers, we're going to learn more complex features. children.title: Handwritten Digit Classification Process
children.summary: This segment introduces a handwritten digit classification example using black and white images. Each pixel is represented by values of 1 (ink) and 0 (no ink). The images are resized to 9 by 7 pixels, totaling 63 pixels per image. The serialization method is explained, where pixels are organized from top left to bottom right, facilitating classification into 10 classes for the digits 0 through 9.

children.title: Handwritten Digit Classification Example
children.summary: The segment introduces a simple example of handwritten digit classification using black and white images, where pixels are represented by values of 1 (ink) and 0 (no ink). It explains the process of resizing the images to 9 by 7 pixels, resulting in 63 pixels per image. The serialization method is described, where pixels are organized from top left to bottom right, allowing for classification into 10 different classes representing the digits 0 through 9.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I'm going to start with a simple example or a simple data set. So the test that I'm going to walk you through is handwritten digit classification. Assumes that, assume that there is all of these black and white images of handwritten digits, black and white means that I have pixels that are either they have ink. I'm going to show those pixels with the value of one or they don't have ink or white and I I should have, but the value of 0. So what I'm going to do is I have assuming I have one handwritten digit or image of the handwritten digit. Let's assume that I actually go ahead and say that I resize all of these images so that these images are 9 by 7. So I have 63 different pixels. So the way that we actually are going to process this kind of image in order to classify it into 10 different classes. I have class 0, class 1, all the way to class of digit 9 is that I will serialize all of these pixels. I would say that I'm going to take all of the first row. I'm going to say that that's input 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7. And then I'm going to take the second row. I would say that that's pixel input 8, 9, and then so on and so forth. So we will serialize it from the top left to the bottom right. Video: 1: v

children.title: Neuron Functionality and Feature Detection in Neural Networks
children.summary: In digit classification using neural networks, the output probabilities for classes zero to nine are derived from 60 input pixels. A specific neuron in the second layer is highlighted for its strong connections to the first seven pixels, indicating its role in detecting features from the first row of the input image. Neuron activation in the first layer occurs through strong summation of input values, allowing detection of specific features like horizontal lines or dark areas, with the importance of bias terms also noted. The segment further explains neural connections, emphasizing the distinction between strong and weak connections, which influences the features a neuron learns. Attention in deep learning is discussed, illustrating how each neuron acts as a feature detector, essential for classifying handwritten digits, with examples of how different shapes correspond to specific digits.

children.title: Neuron Functionality in Digit Classification
children.summary: In a digit classification task using neural networks, the output consists of probabilities for each class from zero to nine based on 60 input pixels. The discussion focuses on the role of a specific neuron in the second layer, which has strong connections to the first seven pixels of the input image. This neuron is suggested to detect features related to the first row of pixels, as weak connections to other pixels indicate they do not influence the neuron's output. The significance of these connections in feature detection is explored.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I have 60 tree inputs instead of two and they're going to go into multiple different layers and so on and so forth. And my task is going to do classification. So the output probability or the output labels that I have here is that what is the probability of image belonging to class zero? What is the probability of the image belonging to class one all the way to image belonging to class nine? Okay, so that's a simple task that I have. But hypothetically, if I want to understand why did I have multiple different layers, and what does each one of these neurons do? Let's assume that I'm going to take a look at this neuron in the second layer, and I would say that, okay, this neuron is connected to the input neurons, and I would say that my neuron has a strong connection to the first seven pixels. and it has weak connections to the rest of the pixels. What kind of feature is this neuron detecting? Again, when the connection is weak, that means that irrespective of what you put in the input, it's just going to be zeroed out. So assume that all of these weights are weights that are very close to zero, irrespective of these pixel values, whether they're equal to 1 or zero, they're going to be multiplied with zero so they're not going to have any impact on the output that this neuron creates, right? looks like that it's paying attention to the first seven pixels. Right? What are those seven first seven pixels that I have? I said that these first seven pixels are the first row or pixels in the first row that I have. What does it mean for my neuron paying attention to the first row? Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Neuron Activation and Feature Detection in Images
children.summary: Neurons in the first layer of a neural network activate based on strong summation of input values, detecting specific features such as horizontal black lines or dark areas in an image. For example, a neuron may have strong weights for certain pixels while ignoring others, indicating its focus on specific image regions for tasks like digit classification. The discussion also touches on the consideration of bias terms in neuron activation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It means that this neuron is only going to be activated if there is a strong summation of activation in the inputs meaning that it's going to output one if most of these pixel values are equal to one meaning that if most of those pixel values are black right? That means that I want to have a horizontal black line at the top. So that's what this feature is actually detecting. This is hypothetically, this is like assuming that after the training, you look at one moon neuron and then you see that there is a strong positive waste of recene. So the other thing that we can actually take a look at is that, okay, what if this neuron, this, this is another neuron, has positive connection to, for example, the first three pixels, then it has zero, the weight of zero connected to the, to the rest of the four, and then another three is strong weights and then another four weak weights and so on and so forth. So again, And that looks like that. We want to have a strong signal here in the first tree. Then we're going to ignore everything else right here. Then we want to have another strong signal right here. Ignore everything else. The strong signal. Ignore everything else. What does that give us? That is actually giving us what we're interested in is a dark circle or a dark area in the top left corner. So that's how you can interpret what a neuron is actually doing. And in the first layer, neurons are going to be very simple. They're looking at the input pixels and they're trying to find features in different regions of the image so that those features can be used for the final task of digit classification. Question. There's a good question that is this assuming that there is bias term or not? Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Neural Connections and Feature Learning
children.summary: The segment explains the concept of neural connections in a simplified manner, focusing on the absence of a bias term. It describes the distinction between strong and weak connections, emphasizing that weak connections are ignored while strong connections are prioritized. This relationship is crucial as it determines the type of feature a neuron learns based on the strength of its connections.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is like a very simple explanation of a feature. Assume that there is no bias term and just assume that those are the only kind of connections that I have. I have a bunch of strong connections and a bunch of weak connections and the idea of weak connection is that those neurons or those inputs, whatever they say, we ignore. We don't listen to them. We don't look at them and then we pay attention to the strong connections. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Feature Detection in Neural Networks
children.summary: The concept of attention in deep learning relates to identifying specific features within data. Each neuron acts as a simple feature detector, and a complex set of these detectors is necessary for tasks like classifying handwritten digits. For instance, detecting vertical lines may indicate a higher probability of the digit being '1', while horizontal lines suggest the digit '2'. Additionally, recognizing circular shapes helps differentiate digits like '6', '8', or '9'. The connections between a neuron and its inputs determine the specific features it learns.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Right, so the term attention that you'll hear probably in deep learning is kind of coming from the same idea that like which area or what feature we're paying attention to So here we're picking dark area in the specific region, so that's kind of generally one feature detector. So you can think about each one of those neurons as actually small feature detectors that we have. But each one of these features are actually very simple. What we need is a very complicated set of features, or a complicated set of feature detectors in order to complete the job. So for example, if you're looking at all of the different variety of handwritten digits that we have. In order to be able to do this 9-way classification or 10-way classification that we have, we need to have, for example, detect vertical lines. If there is a vertical line, there is more probability that, for example, a digit is 1 compared to a digit being equal to 0. That's how you can think about why those features are important. For example, we might need to detect horizontal lines. If you have a horizontal line, it's more likely for your digit to be, for example, a digit of two compared to a digit of one. So you can think about those features being very important in aggregate when you're trying to find patterns to distinguish between the classes. And then obviously, we can also find circles. Like one doesn't have circle, but two or nine or six, they can have circles in those images. But the issue is that I have a small feature detectors. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Feature Detection in Neural Networks
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the ability of neural networks to detect specific features such as horizontal lines, vertical lines, and circular areas in images. It emphasizes that these detections are tied to specific pixel positions, raising the question of position invariance, which refers to the network's ability to recognize features regardless of their location in the image.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I showed you that, OK, I can detect a horizontal line. I can detect a vertical line, I can detect kind of a circle or black area in the rich region. But they are always positioned specific, because I was connecting to the first seven pixels. I was connecting to the second seven pixels, for example. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Achieving Position Invariance in Neural Networks
children.summary: Position invariance in neural networks can be achieved by using multiple layers of neurons that detect lower-level features, such as horizontal and vertical lines. The first layer consists of weak learners that identify these features, while subsequent layers combine this information to form more complex detectors. For example, a neuron in the second layer can recognize the presence of a horizontal line without being tied to a specific position, allowing it to ignore irrelevant features. This layered approach enables the detection of various shapes and patterns, enhancing the network's ability to generalize across different positions in the input.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So it was always position dependent. How can I actually make it position invariant? In order to make a position in variance, so you can think about having the input layer going into the first layer, the first layer being a bunch of different weak learners. Or they are detecting lower level features for us. So I can build for example one neuron that detects the first line or first horizontal line second horizontal line third horizontal line and so on and so forth. and then I can add position invariance by taking or between them. I can say that do you have a horizontal line at all or not and we know how we can build or gate. So that's why we need to add layers. You can see that the neuron that I have in the second layer is more complex than the neuron in the first layer. Because the second one, not only that it detects the horizontal line but is also not positioned as specific. I can do exactly the same thing with other features. I can detect vertical lines anywhere, I can detect circles, any verse on and so forth. And then what I can do is I can actually add another layer that takes into this information and says that okay, like, What if I have a vertical line and I have circles but I don't have a horizontal line. I'm not paying attention to horizontal line. I'm just going to ignore whatever you say. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

leaf_chunk.audio: So it was always position dependent. How can I actually make it position invariant? In order to make a position in variance, so you can think about having the input layer going into the first layer, the first layer being a bunch of different weak learners. Or they are detecting lower level features for us. So I can build for example one neuron that detects the first line or first horizontal line second horizontal line third horizontal line and so on and so forth. and then I can add position invariance by taking or between them. I can say that do you have a horizontal line at all or not and we know how we can build or gate. So that's why we need to add layers. You can see that the neuron that I have in the second layer is more complex than the neuron in the first layer. Because the second one, not only that it detects the horizontal line but is also not positioned as specific. I can do exactly the same thing with other features. I can detect vertical lines anywhere, I can detect circles, any verse on and so forth. And then what I can do is I can actually add another layer that takes into this information and says that okay, like, What if I have a vertical line and I have circles but I don't have a horizontal line. I'm not paying attention to horizontal line. I'm just going to ignore whatever you say.

children.title: Multi-class Classification and Softmax Function
children.summary: Multi-class classification involves normalizing activations to form a probability function, allowing for comparison between different classes. Instead of using independent probabilities from a sigmoid function, a softmax layer is applied to the output layer, converting activations into a set of probabilities. This enables a clearer comparison of class probabilities, such as determining a data point's higher likelihood of belonging to one class over another based on the calculated probabilities.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what kind of digit am I extracting here? This is probably a digit of nine or a digit of six right so that's why then combining all of those with different kinds of combination of weights is going to actually help you to actually do the full classification test. So what I'll do is I will actually skip the next part. So the next part is actually going to talk about why, how we can actually do multi-class classification. The idea is that we have multi-different classes and what we need to do is we take all of these activations. And instead of using those activations directly, we will normalize those activations so that those activations form a probability function. And then you can actually compare the probability of class 0 versus probability of class 1 and so on and so forth. So what's happening is that instead of me passing each one of those activations in the output layer to a sigmoid function and get independent probabilities, then what I'll have is I will actually have something that's called softmax layer. That is actually going to take these activations in the input. It will apply a certain function called softmax function. Again, I'm not going to go over that. That will turn the output layer into a set of probabilities as opposed to individual probabilities. So now we can compare them a little bit more. Concretely, that is more likely for the data point that we have to belong to class 1 as opposed to class 2 because the probability is 88% as opposed to 12%. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Deep Neural Networks and Softmax Layer
children.summary: Deep neural networks increase function complexity by adding multiple hidden layers, allowing for the learning of more non-linear patterns. The softmax layer serves as the output layer for multi-class classification, transforming raw output scores into probabilities.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So. The next piece is kind of simple. The idea of, now you know that the idea of deep neural network is that we add multiple different hidden layers. If we add multiple different hidden layers, the complexity of the function that we're going to train is going to increase. And then we are going to be able to learn more non-linear patterns. children.title: Deep Neural Networks and Function Approximation
children.summary: The segment discusses the universality theorem, which states that a neural network with one sufficiently large hidden layer can approximate any function mapping an n-dimensional input to an m-dimensional output. However, as the complexity of the function increases, the size of the hidden layer also increases exponentially. To address this, deep neural networks are proposed, where multiple hidden layers are stacked to reduce the number of neurons needed, leading to fewer parameters and simpler training processes. This approach also requires less data for training. The segment also references visualizations related to logic circuits, illustrating that functions can be built with fewer gates when layers are stacked.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The general idea comes from universality term in universality term. They're saying that we can learn any function that maps an end-dimensional input to an M-dimensional output. Using one hidden one neural network with one hidden layer that is large enough so the look the neural that that hidden layer should be large enough for us to be able to realize that function. But the idea is that if we go ahead and want to realize any complex function, the size of that layer is going to grow very big and it's going to be a very exponential. So the idea that we'll use is we'll actually try to break down this entire fact, hidden layer, and stack them together and make it a deep neural network. And if you actually go ahead and take a look at the visualization that I link here, it's very useful for you to get some ideas. So we use ideas from logic circuit. Then in logic circuit, we can actually build any function with two layers. But if we actually stack them into multiple different layers, the number of gates that we're actually going to use is going to be reduced. So that's also the same thing that we know we can realize any function with one hidden layer of large size or large number of neurons. But if we actually stack them together, we're going to actually require less number of neurons and less number of neurons means less number of parameters that we have. If we have less number of parameters, their training is going to be simpler, and then we're going to require less data to do the training for us. So I want to quickly move us to the demo. Video: 1: v

leaf_chunk.audio: The general idea comes from universality term in universality term. They're saying that we can learn any function that maps an end-dimensional input to an M-dimensional output. Using one hidden one neural network with one hidden layer that is large enough so the look the neural that that hidden layer should be large enough for us to be able to realize that function. But the idea is that if we go ahead and want to realize any complex function, the size of that layer is going to grow very big and it's going to be a very exponential. So the idea that we'll use is we'll actually try to break down this entire fact, hidden layer, and stack them together and make it a deep neural network. And if you actually go ahead and take a look at the visualization that I link here, it's very useful for you to get some ideas. So we use ideas from logic circuit. Then in logic circuit, we can actually build any function with two layers. But if we actually stack them into multiple different layers, the number of gates that we're actually going to use is going to be reduced. So that's also the same thing that we know we can realize any function with one hidden layer of large size or large number of neurons. But if we actually stack them together, we're going to actually require less number of neurons and less number of neurons means less number of parameters that we have. If we have less number of parameters, their training is going to be simpler, and then we're going to require less data to do the training for us. So I want to quickly move us to the demo.

children.title: Overview of Neural Networks and PyTorch Demo
children.summary: The segment introduces the upcoming demonstration on Neural Networks using PyTorch, highlighting key topics such as artificial neurons, their expressiveness, and their role as feature detectors. It covers the limitations of logistic regression, the implementation of logistic regression and a simple neural network in PyTorch, and the training of a neural network with a softmax output layer for multi-class classification tasks.

children.title: History and Evolution of Neural Networks
children.summary: The discussion covers the historical use of neural networks and related technologies, starting from the early 1990s when they were utilized in applications like mail messaging and ATMs. It highlights a period in the early 2000s when research in this area declined due to ineffective models that couldn't scale to complex functions. The narrative transitions to a resurgence in the 2010s, emphasizing the current reliance on neural networks for various applications, while noting that Scikit-learn, a popular package, does not focus on neural networks and is not used in this part of the course.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I love this stuff. I should get some background stories. It's kind of fun. So we're playing digits. Did you know we were using this stuff in like the early 1990s? In fact, when you're shipping, you know, sending a message in mail, or if you went to an ATM, that's an automated teller machine. It's a place where you stick your card and get cash out of. If you went to one of those, if you went to a positive check, which is something people used to do, you would read the check using this technology in the 90s. Early 90s is crazy. It's this old stuff. There was a period in time when it was terror like... That was a grad student. Early 2000s, when we didn't research this stuff, it wasn't trendy because the models didn't work. There's a classic result that said we couldn't scale these things to really, really complex function out making these things exponentially big. And then I guess in the 2010s, 2015 era, we switched back to everything as a neural net. And so now we do almost all the things we do in neural nets today. Scikit-learn is a cool package. It doesn't do neural nets, so. We don't use that for this part of the class. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Introduction to Binary Classification Task
children.summary: The segment introduces a binary classification task using a dataset generated with Scikit-learn's make_circles, which consists of two concentric circles representing different classes. The focus is on creating a dataset that is not linearly separable, setting the stage for further exploration in neural networks.

Binary Classification Task on Circles Dataset
Step 0: Getting the Data
First, let's generate some data. We will use Scikit-learn's make_circles to create a dataset that isn't linearly separable.
The dataset consists of two concentric circles where the color of each point represents which class it belongs to.
[ ] :
from sklearn.datasets import make_circles
X, y = make_circles(n_samples=1000, noise=0.2, factor=0.5, random_state=42)
data_fig = go. FigureWidget()
data_fig.add_trace(go. Scatter(x=X[y == 0, 0], y=X[y == 0, 1],
mode='markers', marker=dict(color='red ]),
name='0'))
data_fig.add_trace(go. Scatter(x=X[y == 1, 0], y=X[y == 1, 1],
mode='markers', marker=dict(color='blue'),
name='1'))
data_fig.update_layout(width=500, height=500,
xaxis_range=[-2, 2], yaxis_range=[-2, 2],
xaxis_title='Feature 1',
yaxis_title='Feature 2',
title='make_circles Dataset')
data_fig.show()
print("The first 5 training datapoints:", X[ :5])
print("The labels for the first 5 datapoints:", y[ : 5])

children.title: Building a Neural Network for Non-linearly Separable Data
children.summary: The segment discusses setting up a toy dataset that is not linearly separable and the rationale for using 32-bit precision tensors in PyTorch, particularly for GPU acceleration. It introduces the logistic regression model using a linear layer, which consists of a bias and weights. The segment also mentions generating a dataset with Scikit-learn's make_circles function, creating two concentric circles to illustrate binary classification.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So a lot of this visualization code is way out of scope for this class. But if you want to learn about how to do fun visualizations, check it out. Let's just go ahead and run through this and set up our toy data set. So here's our new toy data set. Is this linearly separable? No. So let's go ahead and build a neural network to process this data. Perdelo, sir. I was performing a create tensors. One thing I was kind of neat here, I'm using these 32-bit tensors, these 32-bit precision tensors. The reason we work with tensors in PyTorch is often actually also been working on a GPU. I'm not showing this today, but it's very easy to tell PyTorch to move this tensor to a GPU, and then when I do this matrix multiply all this math, it will all run on my GPU many, many times faster. There's another reason we use tensors. Alright, time for the set. Choop. All right, so logistic regression model. Here I'm going to use the linear layer. So linear layer is a bias plus these weights. So it's a standard layer we've been talking about the whole way through. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

Binary Classification Task on Circles Dataset
Step 0: Getting the Data
First, let's generate some data. We will use Scikit-learn's make_circles to create a dataset that isn't linearly separable.
The dataset consists of two concentric circles where the color of each point represents which class it belongs to.
[ ] :
from sklearn.datasets import make_circles
X, y = make_circles(n_samples=1000, noise=0.2, factor=0.5, random_state=42)
data_fig = go. FigureWidget()
data_fig.add_trace(go. Scatter(x=X[y == 0, 0], y=X[y == 0, 1],
mode='markers', marker=dict(color='red ]),
name='0'))
data_fig.add_trace(go. Scatter(x=X[y == 1, 0], y=X[y == 1, 1],
mode='markers', marker=dict(color='blue'),
name='1'))
data_fig.update_layout(width=500, height=500,
xaxis_range=[-2, 2], yaxis_range=[-2, 2],
xaxis_title='Feature 1',
yaxis_title='Feature 2',
title='make_circles Dataset')
data_fig.show()
print("The first 5 training datapoints:", X[ :5])
print("The labels for the first 5 datapoints:", y[ : 5])

def make_tensors(X, y) :
from torch. utils. data import random_split, TensorDataset
data = TensorDataset(torch. tensor(X, dtype=torch.float32),
torch. tensor(y, dtype=torch.long))
torch. manual_seed(140)*
train_data, test_data = random_split(data, [0.8, 0.2])
return train_data, test_data
training_data, test_data = make_tensors(X, y)

# Logistic Regression Model
class LogisticRegressionModel(nn.Module) :
def __ init __ (self):
super(LogisticRegressionModel, self) ._ init __ ()
self. linear = nn.Linear(2, 1)
I
def forward(self, x):
p = torch.sigmoid(self.linear(x))
return torch.cat([1 - p, p], dim=1)
Step 2: Define the Loss
Here we are using cross entropy loss since we made the model return a probability for each class.

return torch.cat([1 - p, p], dim=1)
Step 2: Define the Loss
Here we are using cross entropy loss since we made the model return a probability for each class.

def make_tensors(X, y) :
from torch. utils. data import random_split, TensorDataset
data = TensorDataset(torch. tensor(X, dtype=torch.float32),
torch. tensor(y, dtype=torch.long))
torch. manual_seed(140)*
train_data, test_data = random_split(data, [0.8, 0.2])
return train_data, test_data
training_data, test_data = make_tensors(X, y)

children.title: Building Neural Network Architectures in PyTorch
children.summary: When constructing networks, individual parameters are not typically written; instead, users define layers like linear and convolutional layers or transformer blocks in a concise manner. This allows for the representation of complex architectures as single layers within a model. In the initialization functions, models are defined simply, such as a registered regression model with a linear layer. The forward function is crucial as it computes the output of the model by processing input data and utilizing defined parameters. The discussion also highlights the use of cross-entropy loss, transitioning from binary to multi-class predictions, where both class probabilities are computed.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: When we build networks, we don't typically write individual parameters today. We say, I want a linear layer, I want a con layer, I want these transformer blocks. So it's very easy to write one function. Like that to express really interesting architectures. And so there's architecture representing the fact that full collections of networks I can put as just one layer inside of my model. And so when you look at PyTorch code for, you know, state-the-art models, they might have like five or six lines where it says like this giant architecture connected to this other giant architecture. That's my model. So in these init functions, I'm defining the thing that my models have a linear layer, that's it, this is a registered regression model starting small. The forward function is where all the magic happens. This is the function that would take an input. And when I write this word function I'm telling the model I'm telling pie torch how to compute the output of this model it's building up the architecture not just you know defining the parameters but actually using them to make a producer result from some input. We're using cross-entry P loss. We use the binary cross-entry loss before. Little subtleties. So before I was predicting one number, the probability of class one, now I'm going to start to predict the probability of class zero if you end, because I want to do multi-class. In binary, I have two classes. So I'm going to predict two probabilities. So you go back to my forward function, and compute p, but it would turn two numbers, one minus p and p. children.title: Cross-Entity Loss and Optimizer Techniques
children.summary: The segment discusses cross-entity loss applicable across multiple dimensions and classes, detailing the implementation of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) in state-yard systems with data loaders for efficient data batch management and GPU processing. It includes a code review covering the looping over epochs, data batch retrieval, and a logistic regression model in PyTorch that uses cross-entropy loss for optimization. Additionally, the segment explains the optimization process using SGD, model prediction computation from input data, loss evaluation, and the backward step for gradient descent. The Adam Optimizer is introduced as a stable parameter update method, with a brief overview of the logistic regression code setup, data loaders, and training loop.

children.title: Cross-Entity Loss and Optimizer Overview
children.summary: The discussion covers the use of cross-entity loss applicable to any number of dimensions and classes. It highlights the implementation of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) in state-yard systems, utilizing data loaders to efficiently manage data batches and GPU processing. The code review includes looping over epochs, pulling batches of data, and an overview of a logistic regression model defined in PyTorch, which incorporates cross-entropy loss for optimization.

def plot_decision_boundary_pytorch(model, num_points=100, probs=True):
# Generate a grid of points
xx, yy = torch.meshgrid(torch.linspace(-4, 4, num_points),
torch. linspace(-4, 4, num_points),
indexing='ij')
grid = torch.cat([xx.reshape(-1, 1), yy.reshape(-1, 1)], dim=1)
with torch.no_grad():
preds = model (grid)
num_classes = preds. shape [1]
if num_classes > 2: # support for multiclass
preds = torch. argmax(preds, axis=1).reshape(xx. shape). T
return go. Contour(x=xx[ :, 0], y=yy[0], z=preds,
contours=dict(start=0, end=num_classes, size=1),
colorscale=[px.colors. qualitative. Plotly[i] for i in range(num_classes)],
Simple

test_dataset,
model, loss_fn,

batch_size=64,
learning_rate = 0.01,
nepochs=50,
sleep_time=0.2):
import time
from torch. utils. data import DataLoader
# Create a dataloader for training
train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True)
test_loader = DataLoader(test_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=False)
# Define the optimizer (this is the update rule)
optimizer = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), learning_rate)
test_loss_curve = []
for epoch in range (nepochs):
# Loop through all the batches
for batch, (X, y) in enumerate(train_loader):
# Zero the gradients to start the next step
optimizer.zero_grad()
# Compute prediction and Loss
pred = model (X)
loss = loss_fn(pred, y)
# Backpropagation (compute the gradient)
loss.backward()
# Update the parameters using the optimizer's update rule
optimizer.step()

# Loop through all the batches

# Zero the gradients to start the next step
optimizer.zero_grad()
# Compute prediction and Loss
pred = model (X)
loss = loss_fn(pred, y)
# Backpropagation (compute the gradient)
loss.backward()
# Update the parameters using the optimizer's update rule
optimizer.step()

def plot_decision_boundary_pytorch(model, num_points=100, probs=True):
# Generate a grid of points
xx, yy = torch.meshgrid(torch.linspace(-4, 4, num_points),
torch. linspace(-4, 4, num_points),
indexing='ij')
grid = torch.cat([xx.reshape(-1, 1), yy.reshape(-1, 1)], dim=1)
with torch.no_grad():
preds = model (grid)
num_classes = preds. shape [1]
if num_classes > 2: # support for multiclass
preds = torch. argmax(preds, axis=1).reshape(xx. shape). T
return go. Contour(x=xx[ :, 0], y=yy[0], z=preds,
contours=dict(start=0, end=num_classes, size=1),
colorscale=[px.colors. qualitative. Plotly[i] for i in range(num_classes)],

def plot_decision_boundary_pytorch(model, num_points=100, probs=True):
# Generate a grid of points
xx, yy = torch.meshgrid(torch.linspace(-4, 4, num_points),
torch. linspace(-4, 4, num_points),
indexing='ij')
grid = torch.cat([xx.reshape(-1, 1), yy.reshape(-1, 1)], dim=1)
with torch.no_grad():
preds = model (grid)
num_classes = preds. shape [1]
if num_classes > 2: # support for multiclass
preds = torch. argmax(preds, axis=1).reshape(xx. shape). T
return go. Contour(x=xx[ :, 0], y=yy[0], z=preds,
contours=dict(start=0, end=num_classes, size=1),
colorscale=[px.colors. qualitative. Plotly[i] for i in range(num_classes)],

import time
from torch. utils. data import DataLoader
# Create a dataloader for training
train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True)
test_loader = DataLoader(test_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=False)
# Define the optimizer (this is the update rule)
optimizer = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), learning_rate)
test_loss_curve = []
for epoch in range (nepochs):
# Loop through all the batches
for batch, (X, y) in enumerate(train_loader):
# Zero the gradients to start the next step
optimizer.zero_grad()
# Compute prediction and Loss
pred = model (X)
loss = loss_fn(pred, y)
# Backpropagation (compute the gradient)
loss.backward()
# Update the parameters using the optimizer's update rule
optimizer.step()

children.title: Gradient Descent and Adam Optimizer in Neural Networks
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of using stochastic gradient descent (SGD) for optimizing a model. It explains how the model's predictions are computed using input data (X) and how the loss is evaluated. The focus is on the backward step where gradient descent occurs, specifically how the system computes the gradient automatically. The Adam Optimizer is introduced as a stable method for updating model parameters, incorporating additional parameters for stability. The segment also includes a brief overview of the code used for logistic regression, including the setup for data loaders and the training loop.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And that's going to be a subset of my data, a little block of X and Ys. I'm shuffling it, doing SGD. I set my optimizer to zero so I'm initializing optimization step. I compute a prediction by just running my Model on X. and I can beat the loss. Where is gradient descent happening? This is gradient descent. in the backwards step. the lost. I'm computing the gradient. This is Calculus in 2025, right? We just say backward. This system automatically does all the math to compute the gradient. The optimizer. Is going to then take that gradient and do the weight update to my model parameters. Here I'm using the Adam Optimizer, which is Stady Optimizer, so it's Stady Art Code. This optimizer is to cast a gradient, but it's setting a bunch of extra parameters to make sure this thing is stable. Now we can try running this. That's my code. Moving through it now you can see... Oops, I... You can see... trying to solve a logistic regression model for this data set. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

sleep_time=0.2):
import time
from torch. utils.data import DataLoader
# Create a dataloader for training
train_loader = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True)
test_loader = DataLoader(test_dataset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=False)
# Define the optimizer (this is the update rule)
optimizer = optim. Adam(model.parameters(), learning_rate)
test_loss_curve = []
for epoch in range (nepochs):
# Loop through all the batches
for batch, (X, y) in enumerate(train_loader):
# Zero the gradients to start the next step
optimizer.zero_grad()
# Compute prediction and Loss
pred = model (X)
loss = loss_fn(pred, y)
# Backpropagation (compute the gradient)
loss. backward()
# Update the parameters using the optimizer's update rule
optimizer.step()
# Evaluate the model on the test data
# In practice, we often do this in batches too since the data is too big to fit in memory
with torch.no_grad():
test_loss_sum = 0.0

children.title: Logistic Regression vs. Neural Network Structure
children.summary: The segment compares the performance of a logistic regression model, noting its inadequacy despite decreasing loss, and transitions to constructing a neural network with two hidden layers. The network features a structure of 2 by 8 and 8 by 8 neurons, where two inputs are transformed into eight outputs via a linear layer and tanh non-linearity. The output from the first hidden layer feeds into the second, producing another set of eight outputs, which are then combined into a single output using a sigmoid function to generate a probability for class distinction, highlighting the necessity for a non-linear decision boundary.

children.title: Logistic Regression and Neural Network Overview
children.summary: The segment discusses the performance of a logistic regression model, noting that while the loss is decreasing, the model remains inadequate for making accurate predictions. The speaker indicates a transition to building a neural network with two hidden layers, specifically a structure of 2 by 8 and 8 by 8, which will ultimately be reduced to a single output layer.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The loss is going down. Why is it going down? I can't fit this state of logistic regression. A good review for the exam. I'm changing the logistic regression model so that it is flat as possible saying I don't know. Probably 0.5 all the way through here. It could be class 1 or class 2. So I use logistic regression and I fit something that's truly non-non-separable. The best I can guess, the best model is just, I don't know, .5 the Hueh Hueh. So there is an improvement. But still a terrible model, right? Makes bad predictions. That's Dylan Earl Network. So I'm going to build a neural network with two hidden layers. It's apparent that our linear decision boundary won't cut it for this data. Let's try to build a simple neural network that can distinguish between these
two classes.
[ ]:
class NeuralNetworkModel1(nn.Module) :
def __ init __ (self):
super() .__ init __ ()
self.hidden1 = nn.Linear(4, 8)
self.hidden2 = nn.Linear(8, 8)
self.output = nn. Linear(8, 1)
def forward(self, x):
X = torch.tanh(self.hidden1(x))
x = torch.tanh(self.hidden2(x))
p = torch.sigmoid(self.output(x))
return torch. cat([1 - p, p], dim=1)
L

return torch. cat([1 - p, p], dim=1)

children.title: Building a Neural Network with Two Hidden Layers
children.summary: A neural network is constructed with two hidden layers, each consisting of eight neurons. The process begins with two inputs that are transformed into eight outputs through a linear layer followed by a tanh non-linearity. The output from the first hidden layer serves as the input to the second hidden layer, which also produces eight outputs. Finally, these outputs are combined into a single output using a sigmoid function to yield a probability between 0 and 1. The model is designed to distinguish between two classes, emphasizing the need for a neural network to achieve a non-linear decision boundary.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So it's a two hidden layers I take two inputs, make an 8, 8 neuron layer, beat an 8 neuron, 8 input to 8 output neuron layer and then combine those results. I apply that hidden layer. That's just a linear layer. Then I put a tan H non-linearity. I could use any number of non-linearities tan H is another one. It's like a sigmoid bit goes from negative 1 to 1. Then I take the output of that, that's the output my first in layer, I call that x again, I make that the input to my next in layer. And I take its output and make that the input to my next hidden layer. Output is the final layer that's combining those into one number. I take the sigmoid to make that a zero one thing, a probability, and now I've computed a neural net. So I did. So input through a non-linear, through a, a, a, a layer that takes two inputs and produces eight outputs. That's my new output. Now I have eight dimensions, eight hidden outputs. I feed that into something that's eight by eight. So it takes eight things in, produce another eight. And I take those eight and collect them back to one. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

children.title: Weight Initialization Methods in Neural Networks
children.summary: Weight initialization is essential for effective training in neural networks, requiring small, non-zero starting weights unlike logistic regression, which can use zero initialization. Xavier initialization is a recommended approach, setting weights to a normal distribution with a mean of 0 and variance based on the number of input and output units. A code example illustrates the implementation of weight initialization using PyTorch's nn.init functions.

children.title: Weight Initialization in Neural Networks
children.summary: Initializing weights in neural networks is crucial for effective training. Unlike logistic regression, where weights can be initialized to zero without issue, neural networks require small, non-zero starting weights to ensure optimization. Xavier initialization is a recommended method, setting weights to a normal distribution with mean 0 and variance based on the number of input and output units. A code example demonstrates how to implement weight initialization using PyTorch's nn.init functions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: When we build neural nets, we can't initialize the weights to just zero or one. It won't work. In fact, you could think through it by make all my weight zero what happens to these models. I can no longer optimize them. So we tend to initialize them to something that's close to zero, so it's a stable optimization process, but not truly zero. There's a whole field of how to better optimize your models or how to better initialize them. I have a model. I can initialize my weights for that model and I can use the same logistic regression or optimization code I use for logistic regression for this neural net. Same code. class NeuralNetworkModel1(nn. Module) :
def
init_(self):
super() ._ init_()
self. hidden1 = nn.Linear(2,8)
self. hidden2 = nn.Linear(8, 8)
self .output = nn Linear(8, 1)
def forward(self, x):
x = torch.tanh(self.hidden1(x))
x = torch.tanh(self.hidden2(x))
return torch.cat([1 - p, p], dim=1)
G
How we initialize the weights can have a big impact on how well our model trains. With logistic regression we can set all the weights to zero and be
fine. With a neural network we cannot. Why? However, small starting weights are generally a good idea. Xavier initialization is a popular method for
initializing weights in a neural network. It sets the weights to be normally distributed with mean 0 and variance 2/(number of input units + number of
output units). PyTorch has a function for this in torch. nn. init .

if isinstance(layer, nn. Linear):

init_(self):

self. hidden2 = nn.Linear(8, 8)

children.title: Neural Network Training and Visualization Techniques
children.summary: A three hidden layer neural network is trained in real-time for classification, demonstrating decision boundaries and a decrease in cross-entropy loss to 0.45, indicating better model fitting. An interactive module allows users to modify activation functions, hidden layers, and neuron counts, showing how these changes, along with learning rates and batch sizes, impact optimization and overall model performance.

children.title: Real-time Neural Network Classification and Loss Visualization
children.summary: A three hidden layer neural network is being trained in real time to classify a dataset. The decision boundaries are observed as the model optimizes, with the loss function, specifically cross-entropy loss, decreasing to 0.45, indicating improved model fitting. The segment highlights the transition probabilities between classes as the model learns.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This is the cool stuff. So this is now we're running in real time. It's trying to classify this data. These are the decision boundaries you can zoom out a little bit so it's going on. This is decision boundary it's learning to try to classify this. This is the loss as it goes in real time. So you know a little bit. You can see it's getting closer and closer to fitting the data. So this is my three hidden layer neural network optimizing for this data set. And when I am done. which is going slowly. When I'm done, I should have something that is pretty close to fitting my data. You super cool, right? You'll notice that probably transitions are some uncertainty of zoom in here that we can Zoom in here. There's some transition that Z values my probability as I go from class zero, Z is zero. I'll over here to class one, getting higher probability, being class one. You saw my loss. This is the cross-century loss. Is it decreased? All the way to 0.45.
