children.title: Interactive Neural Network Visualization
children.summary: An interactive neural network module allows users to experiment with various configurations, such as different activation functions, the number of hidden layers, and the number of neurons in each layer. Users can observe how these changes affect optimization outcomes, including the impact of adjusting learning rates and batch sizes on model performance. The discussion emphasizes the importance of these parameters in training neural networks effectively.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that is one example of a simple architecture. You can play with this offline. Here's a neural network module, a narysexure. This is a very good one. This module lets you pick different activation functions, different number of hidden layers, number of neurons in each hidden layer. these and see how they fail or succeed. So this is now an interactive visualization. So I can change this as two hidden layers. I can say I want. Let's do three hidden layers. Can run the optimization again. It's pretty good. I can change the learning rate and batch sizes. You know, we talked about these in class because you need to know them if I set them wrong. But this high maybe I said the batch size low what's gonna happen. It's gonna go all over the place. Can optimize. Right. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

# Function to dynamically create a neural network
class CustomNeuralNetwork(nn.Module) :
def _init_(self, input_size, output_size, layers, activation_functions):
super() .__ init __ ()
self. layers = nn. ModuleList()
self. activations = []
# Create the Layers dynamically
current_size = input_size
for i, layer_size in enumerate(layers):
self. layers. append(nn.Linear(current_size, layer_size))
I
self. activations. append(activation_functions[i])
current_size = layer_size
# Add the final output Layer
self. layers. append(nn. Linear(current_size, output_size))
self. activations. append('sigmoid' ) # Output activation is sigmoid for binary classification
def forward(self, x):
for i, layer in enumerate(self. layers[ :- 1]): # Apply activation for all but the Last Layer
x = layer(x)
x = self.apply_activation(x, self.activations[i])
X = self.layers[-1](x)
# Final output Layer
p = torch.sigmoid(x) # Apply sigmoid activation for output
return torch. cat([1 - p, p], dim=1)
@staticmethod
def apply_activation(x, activation):
if activation == 'relu':
return torch.relu(x)

# Function to dynamically create a neural network
class CustomNeuralNetwork(nn.Module) :
def _init_(self, input_size, output_size, layers, activation_functions):
super() .__ init __ ()
self. layers = nn. ModuleList()
self. activations = []
# Create the Layers dynamically
current_size = input_size
for i, layer_size in enumerate(layers):
self. layers. append(nn.Linear(current_size, layer_size))
I
self. activations. append(activation_functions[i])
current_size = layer_size
# Add the final output Layer
self. layers. append(nn. Linear(current_size, output_size))
self. activations. append('sigmoid' ) # Output activation is sigmoid for binary classification
def forward(self, x):
for i, layer in enumerate(self. layers[ :- 1]): # Apply activation for all but the Last Layer
x = layer(x)
x = self.apply_activation(x, self.activations[i])
X = self.layers[-1](x)
# Final output Layer
p = torch.sigmoid(x) # Apply sigmoid activation for output
return torch. cat([1 - p, p], dim=1)
@staticmethod
def apply_activation(x, activation):
if activation == 'relu':
return torch.relu(x)

# Function to dynamically create a neural network
class CustomNeuralNetwork(nn.Module) :
def _init_(self, input_size, output_size, layers, activation_functions):
super() .__ init __ ()
self. layers = nn. ModuleList()
self. activations = []
# Create the Layers dynamically
current_size = input_size
for i, layer_size in enumerate(layers):
self. layers. append(nn.Linear(current_size, layer_size))
I
self. activations. append(activation_functions[i])
current_size = layer_size
# Add the final output Layer
self. layers. append(nn. Linear(current_size, output_size))
self. activations. append('sigmoid' ) # Output activation is sigmoid for binary classification
def forward(self, x):
for i, layer in enumerate(self. layers[ :- 1]): # Apply activation for all but the Last Layer
x = layer(x)
x = self.apply_activation(x, self.activations[i])
X = self.layers[-1](x)
# Final output Layer
p = torch.sigmoid(x) # Apply sigmoid activation for output
return torch. cat([1 - p, p], dim=1)
@staticmethod
def apply_activation(x, activation):
if activation == 'relu':
return torch.relu(x)

children.title: Tuning Neural Networks and Future Topics
children.summary: Understanding the parameters for tuning neural network models is crucial for optimal results. Practically, one should download existing models and adjust parameters like batch size and learning rate to enhance learning curves. Resources like the PyTorch site provide demos for efficient classifier building. Upcoming lectures will focus on large language models, including their training and prediction mechanisms.

children.title: Practical Use of Neural Networks and Upcoming Topics
children.summary: Understanding the parameters for tuning neural network models is crucial for achieving optimal results. In practice, one would download existing models and adjust parameters like batch size and learning rate to improve learning curves. Resources such as the PyTorch site offer demos for building classifiers efficiently. Upcoming lectures will cover the workings of large language models, detailing their training and prediction mechanisms.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So these are why we need to know about these parameters for test to cast the gradient. This is how you will set these models. In practice, you will download someone else's model and change these things until you get results that go down and look nice and smooth. If you're looking at a learning curve like this you go, hmm, that is too noisy. Let's make the batch size a little bigger. and it's make the linear a little bit smaller. That's a better learning, better curve. So I'm pretty happy with that result. So this is how you would use neural networks in practice. You'd build these models. You would probably download someone's optimization loop and you'd tune these parameters. As you build more models and you go and learn about PyTorch, you'll learn how to build these individual layers and compose other people's models as building blocks inside of your room. This is stuff you can learn pretty easily if you go to the PyTroTrups site. There are demos in one hour you can build a classifier that does, you know, the digits problem as well as was done in the 1990s and in fact what is used today. So this is the basics of neural networks. If you come to lecture on Thursday, we will talk about how large language models work and I will explain all the details of how we train them and why they're able to make, well, I guess how they make the predictions they make today. So I hope to see you all on Thursday for large language models. Video: 1: Lec 27 - DS100 Fa24 - Neural N X

def _init_(self, input_size, output_size, layers, activation_functions):
super() .__ init __ ()
self.layers = nn.ModuleList()
self. activations = []
# Create the Layers dynamically
current_size = input_size
for i, layer_size in enumerate(layers):
self.layers.append(nn.Linear(current_size, layer_size))
self. activations. append(activation_functions[i])
current_size = layer_size
I
# Add the final output layer
self. layers. append(nn. Linear(current_size, output_size))
self. activations. append('sigmoid' ) # Output activation is sigmoid for binary classification
def forward(self, x):
for i, layer in enumerate(self. layers[ :- 1]): # Apply activation for all but the Last Layer
x = layer(x)
x = self.apply_activation(x, self.activations[i])
X = self.layers[-1](x)
# Final output Layer
p = torch.sigmoid(x) # Apply sigmoid activation for output
return torch.cat([1 - p, p], dim=1)
@staticmethod
def apply_activation(x, activation):
if activation == 'relu':
return torch.relu(x)
elif activation == 'tanh':
return torch. tanh (x)
elif activation == 'sigmoid':

children.title: Neural Network Layer Initialization in PyTorch
children.summary: The segment covers the implementation of a neural network class in PyTorch, focusing on layer initialization and activation functions. It explains the dynamic creation of layers using a for loop to append linear layers and their corresponding activation functions. The final output layer is added with a sigmoid activation function for binary classification. Additionally, the forward method is detailed, which applies the layers and activations sequentially, ending with the sigmoid function to generate the output.

children.title: Neural Network Layer Initialization in PyTorch
children.summary: The segment discusses the implementation of a neural network class in PyTorch, focusing on the initialization of layers and activation functions. It details the dynamic creation of layers using a for loop that iterates through specified layer sizes, appending linear layers and corresponding activation functions to the model. The final output layer is added with a sigmoid activation function for binary classification. The forward method is explained, which applies the layers and activations sequentially, culminating in the application of the sigmoid function to produce the output.
